addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

1	- [Bifrost deploy needs explicit --commit](feedback_bifrost_deploy_commit.md) — Project-level image tags cross-serve services when --commit is omitted; symptom was ops-dashboard URL serving visitor-web HTML.
2	- [auxilio-v2 Bifrost project layout](project_auxilio_v2.md) — Project/env/service IDs, ingress suffix -dev-330299, build args, and which remote each subrepo's Bifrost service tracks.
3	- [User role: pitches Office Ops designs to leadership](user_role.md) — Uses Claude to pressure-test proposals before stakeholder conversations; wants disagreement, not validation.
4	- [Ameya is the cost-sensitive approval stakeholder](project_ameya_stakeholder.md) — "tu tech lagayega, cost aayega" — frame every tech proposal with monthly cost, one-time vs recurring, and the cheaper non-tech alternative first.
5	- [Attendance design: face + CCTV cross-check](project_attendance_design.md) — Fingerprint ruled out (hardware cost); face on phone with CCTV as anti-spoof witness; randomized liveness under consideration.
6	- [Inventory: per-expiry lot tracking + soft-nudge FEFO](project_inventory_lots.md) — Stock tracked as lots; receipts/corrections/dispense all go through inventory_stock_lots. Dispense suggests FEFO but records actual picks.
7	- [Build domains automation-surface-ready, don't enforce](feedback_automation_surface_readiness.md) — New features should keep events typed and side-effects single-call so flow-pack triggers/actions can be added later cheaply; do not register now unless asked.
8	- [Automation editor direction locked: canvas stays + guided UX](project_automation_editor_direction.md) — Rejected recipe-first pivot; layer bespoke action forms, splice-in, sidebar guidance on existing GraphEditor.tsx. Canonical plan: Planning-docs/automation_editor_guided_canvas_overhaul_plan.md.
9	- [Sub-flows deferred, not cancelled](project_subflows_deferred.md) — User explicitly wants this remembered; deferred to focus on waits hardening first. Decisions to lock (payload model, disable, override interaction, permissions) listed in file. Revisit on inventory start, ~20 flow count, or override authoring at scale.
10	- [field-app i18n coverage rules](project_field_app_i18n.md) — Translate words via useT/{token} params; stored values stay canonical English (label-only translation); dates/months left locale-neutral on purpose.
11	- [Dispense allows offline confirm](project_dispense_offline_first.md) — Reversed fail-closed JIT gate; offline/timeout falls back to cached count + outbox reconcile. Confirm blocked only while verifying or on 404 'absent'. Don't re-tighten.
12	- [Background sync cadence is split for cost](project_field_app_sync_cadence.md) — startSync was dormant (never called); now wired in AuthProvider. 30s outbox flush + 5min read pull; don't collapse into one aggressive poll.
13	- [Calendar→visitor-invite scope + access model](project_calendar_visitor_invite.md) — Build magic-address + per-user OAuth now (zero admin cooperation); delegation deferred. One-pipeline producers → CalendarEvent, dedup on tenantId+iCalUID. Marketplace=deferred packaging over same delegation runtime; calendar stays sensitive-scope to avoid CASA.
14	- [Don't run DB tests against the live DB](feedback_no_db_tests_against_live.md) — DATABASE_URL usually points at real dev/prod Postgres; gate DB-backed tests behind explicit opt-in + throwaway DB, never plain `bun test`. No ad-hoc psql/DELETE on live DB without permission.
15	- [Calendar flow consolidation: lifecycle = tracked-event only](project_calendar_flow_consolidation.md) — Invites created ONLY by add-on; OAuth = watch for move/delete sync. Lifecycle needs an owned event: add-on (host makes it) + Slack-self-host (D8: take calendar.events WRITE, create via API). Dashboard/delegated = standalone, no lifecycle (D7: no domain delegation). Drop contacts scope.
16	- [Calendar visitor Chrome extension: desktop surface](project_calendar_chrome_extension.md) — Desktop-only richer surface complementing (not replacing) the add-on; unified by magic address. Option A (auto-banner → one-click native side panel), OAuth in engine, internal-first then public; Calendar-only keeps it approvable without CASA.
17	- [Calendar visitor Add-on: internal-first](project_calendar_visitor_addon.md) — Workspace Add-on for in-event visitor registration, Internal-first (no verification/infra). Thin surface over engine's upsertVisitFromCalendarEvent; narrow currentEventAccess READ_WRITE for two-way without broad OAuth; no Gmail/Drive scopes (avoid CASA). Apps Script thin client.
18	- [Multi-flow invite linking design](project_multi_flow_invite_linking.md) — Lifecycle stage-slots (≤1 flow per trigger, many stages/invite), replace-mode kept, array storage now + playbooks deferred; engine needs no runtime change.
19	- [Cross-office pass classification uses home_location_id](project_home_office_classification.md) — Dropped Google work-location (same-city offices + unmaintained directory); admin sets each employee's home office (reused visitor_locations) in the dashboard; calendar-ingest compares it.
20	- [Reception display guard-login gate + per-location check-in log sheet](project_reception_checkin_log_sheet.md) — Display now PIN-gated (was public URL); per-location check-in Google Sheet via admin OAuth, realtime upsert keyed by session id.
21	- [Design system: Material 3 tokens, not its code](project_design_system_material_tokens.md) — Adopt MD3 tokens/rules in our own tokens.ts; no MUI/Tailwind/paper. Owning tokens keeps future MD4/5 bumps on our schedule. Recorded in root CLAUDE.md.
22	- [All emails auto-branded at sendMail chokepoint](project_email_branding.md) — Shared renderBrandedEmail shell + ensureBrandedHtml in email-provider; lifecycle emails + template editor deferred.
23	- [ops-dashboard Modal + useConfirm/useAlert](project_ops_dashboard_modal_confirm.md) — Shared MD3 dialog replaces native window.confirm/alert; ConfirmProvider hosts one Modal, promise-based hooks keep call sites 1:1. Don't reintroduce native dialogs.
24	- [Envoy competitor teardown](reference_envoy_competitor_teardown.md) — Hands-on signup: same magic-address+add-on+extension shape we chose; issues no pass, no crypto identity check — self-sign-in at staffed iPad + host ping. Validates mint-at-reception; our OTP/rotating-QR substitutes for the receptionist we lack.
25	- [Add-on adopts only this-event passes](project_addon_pass_adoption.md) — Fixed false "Pass sent" on new events; identity recovery now requires the pass be linked to THIS event (isInvitationLinkedToThisEvent). Cross-channel same-day dedup dropped on purpose; don't re-loosen to email+date.
26	- [Add-on email preview: popup kept, blocker fixed](project_addon_email_preview_decision.md) — Rendered-image preview rejected (Chromium cost). Popup-blocker fixed via pre-baked setOpenLink (no round-trip) + 30min token TTL + dropdown onChange persist.
27	- [Bulk invite cancel-all plan](project_bulk_invite_cancel_all.md) — nullable batch_id column (no parent table); cancel-batch loops the per-invite cancel; Option A modal button + Option B grouped queue rows. Plan: Planning-docs/2026-06-23_bulk_invite_cancel_all_plan.md.
28	- [WhatsApp template sender + delivery log](project_whatsapp_template_sender.md) — Typed sendWhatsAppTemplate + WA_TEMPLATES registry (pass_link_ops) + whatsapp_messages status reconciliation. Built, not yet wired to any flow. No flow-editor actions by request.

attachment.content:
- bifrost-platform: Use this skill when an agent needs to deploy applications, manage infrastructure, authenticate, or troubleshoot delivery workflows through the Bifrost CLI. It teaches the agent to use the native `bifrost` binary directly, run a strong preflight for repo/app/platform context, prefer `git` before `gh`, pick sensible project/service defaults, and finish with deployment URLs and actionable diagnostics.
- implementation-planning: Produce an implementation plan so complete and unambiguous that a low- or medium-effort agent can build it mechanically, without re-deriving decisions. Use this whenever the user is planning, designing, scoping, or brainstorming a coding task and intends to implement it later — especially if they say things like "plan this", "design the approach", "before we code", "lay out the steps", or mention switching to a cheaper/faster model for the build. Trigger even when the user just describes a feature and asks how to approach it. The goal is a hand-off artifact: front-load all the reasoning here so implementation is translation, not invention.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Add guest detail editing in auxilio-extension

content.content:
1	# auxilio-extension
2	
3	Google **Chrome extension (MV3)** for Auxilio (Office Ops) — the **desktop** surface for registering office visitors directly inside Google Calendar. It is the richer counterpart to the `auxilio-workspace` Calendar add-on: where the add-on is mobile-capable but lives under the CardService "card ceiling," the extension gives a full React UI (realtime guest updates, per-guest invite toggle, template preview, inline edits, intentional auto-nudge).
4	
5	> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules — they apply here.
6	> Strategy + approval analysis: `../Planning-docs/2026-06-18_calendar_visitor_chrome_extension_decision.md`.
7	> Build plan: the approved plan that scaffolded this repo.
8	
9	## What this is (and isn't)
10	
11	- **Is:** an MV3 extension (WXT + React 19 + TS) that injects a trigger into Google Calendar (web) and renders the registration flow in the **side panel**. A **channel surface / dumb adapter** — it forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.
12	- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, draft curation, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo renders UI and calls the engine.
13	- **Isn't:** mobile. Extensions are desktop-Chrome only. The `auxilio-workspace` add-on remains the mobile surface. Both key off the same **magic address**.
14	
15	## Architecture — three MV3 surfaces
16	
17	- **`entrypoints/background.ts`** (service worker) — the only holder of tokens + network. Mints/caches the Google `id_token` (engine auth) + `access_token` (Calendar read), runs the Calendar API client and the engine `/addon/*` client, keeps per-tab event context, routes messages.
18	- **`entrypoints/calendar.content.ts`** (content script @ `calendar.google.com`) — a **dumb trigger/observer**: detect open event, decode `eid`, inject the "Register a visitor" button (Phase 1) and the magic-address banner (Phase 2). No tokens, no business logic.
19	- **`entrypoints/sidepanel/`** (the app) — React + TanStack Query UI. Pure presentation + server state.
20	
21	```
22	content script ──msg──> background ──HTTPS(id_token)──> office-ops-engine /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*
23	side panel    ──msg──> background ──Calendar API(access_token)──> canonical iCalUID + attendees
24	```
25	
26	## Hard rules
27	
28	1. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail/Drive.** Calendar is *sensitive* (verification, no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → annual CASA assessment. Hard line, same as the add-on.
29	2. **Engine owns the contract.** The extension calls the existing `/api/visitor/calendar/addon/{draft,preview,send,cancel-guest}` routes (ID-token authed). Do **not** add engine signature/scope changes from here. The only engine-side change is env: add this extension's OAuth client id to `AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` (audience allow-list).

file.content:
# auxilio-extension

Google **Chrome extension (MV3)** for Auxilio (Office Ops) — the **desktop** surface for registering office visitors directly inside Google Calendar. It is the richer counterpart to the `auxilio-workspace` Calendar add-on: where the add-on is mobile-capable but lives under the CardService "card ceiling," the extension gives a full React UI (realtime guest updates, per-guest invite toggle, template preview, inline edits, intentional auto-nudge).

> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules — they apply here.
> Strategy + approval analysis: `../Planning-docs/2026-06-18_calendar_visitor_chrome_extension_decision.md`.
> Build plan: the approved plan that scaffolded this repo.

## What this is (and isn't)

- **Is:** an MV3 extension (WXT + React 19 + TS) that injects a trigger into Google Calendar (web) and renders the registration flow in the **side panel**. A **channel surface / dumb adapter** — it forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.
- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, draft curation, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo renders UI and calls the engine.
- **Isn't:** mobile. Extensions are desktop-Chrome only. The `auxilio-workspace` add-on remains the mobile surface. Both key off the same **magic address**.

## Architecture — three MV3 surfaces

- **`entrypoints/background.ts`** (service worker) — the only holder of tokens + network. Mints/caches the Google `id_token` (engine auth) + `access_token` (Calendar read), runs the Calendar API client and the engine `/addon/*` client, keeps per-tab event context, routes messages.
- **`entrypoints/calendar.content.ts`** (content script @ `calendar.google.com`) — a **dumb trigger/observer**: detect open event, decode `eid`, inject the "Register a visitor" button (Phase 1) and the magic-address banner (Phase 2). No tokens, no business logic.
- **`entrypoints/sidepanel/`** (the app) — React + TanStack Query UI. Pure presentation + server state.

```
content script ──msg──> background ──HTTPS(id_token)──> office-ops-engine /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*
side panel    ──msg──> background ──Calendar API(access_token)──> canonical iCalUID + attendees
```

## Hard rules

1. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail/Drive.** Calendar is *sensitive* (verification, no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → annual CASA assessment. Hard line, same as the add-on.
2. **Engine owns the contract.** The extension calls the existing `/api/visitor/calendar/addon/{draft,preview,send,cancel-guest}` routes (ID-token authed). Do **not** add engine signature/scope changes from here. The only engine-side change is env: add this extension's OAuth client id to `AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` (audience allow-list).
3. **Background is the only secret/network holder.** Content script stays a dumb trigger; side panel stays presentation. Keep the thin-adapter boundary.
4. **Send the canonical `iCalUID`** (from the Calendar API `events.get`, not synthesized) so the extension path converges on the same `calendar_event_links` row as `.ics`/OAuth/add-on.
5. **Design: Material 3 tokens, not its code.** Our own `design/tokens.ts` + `global.css` (primary `#92288E`). No MUI/Tailwind/paper. **Icons: lucide-react only**, no Unicode glyphs/emoji in chrome.
6. **Animations subtle, only where needed** — CSS transitions driven by motion tokens; respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.
7. **Internal-first.** Force-installed/unlisted to our Workspace → no OAuth verification yet. Public/commercial = a later packaging + verification exercise (CWS review + OAuth verification), per the decision doc.

## Stack & commands

WXT (MV3 over Vite) + React 19 + TypeScript + TanStack Query (server state) + Zustand (UI/session/event-context) + lucide-react.

```bash
npm install
npm run dev        # WXT dev server; load .output/chrome-mv3 in chrome://extensions (Developer mode → Load unpacked)
npm run build      # production build → .output/chrome-mv3
npm run compile    # tsc --noEmit (type-check)
npm run zip        # packaged zip for store upload
```

Env: copy `.env.example` → `.env` (engine URL, OAuth client id, magic address). `WXT_` vars are inlined at build.

## Versioning

**Bump `package.json` `version` on every change you ship to the browser.** WXT syncs it into `manifest.json` automatically, so `chrome://extensions` shows the loaded build's version. This is the check that a reload actually picked up your change — if the card's version doesn't match what you just built, the old bundle is still loaded (reload there and reopen the side panel). Use semver: patch for fixes, minor for features.

## Deploy / distribution

**Not Bifrost, not EAS.** Internal-first via Chrome admin **force-install** (unlisted, by extension id) or "Load unpacked" for dev. Public later via the Chrome Web Store (payments handled by our own engine; CWS no longer bills).

content.content:
# office-ops-engine

The **central intelligence backend** for Office Ops Intelligence — the system that makes office operations smarter, more efficient, and future-proof. Everything operational flows through here.

> Root context: see `../CLAUDE.md` for the product vision, constraints (cost, user literacy, device capability, infrastructure), and how this subrepo fits the umbrella.
> Repo map: see `REPO_GUIDE.md` for a self-contained, code-grounded description of what's actually in this repo and how it works — written to survive removal of the umbrella/Planning docs. Read it for "what exists and how it runs"; this file is for "how to behave here."

## What this service does

- Ingests messages and events from channels (WhatsApp, Telegram, web forms, PWAs) — channels are input adapters, not the product
- Classifies them into **domains** (visitor, inventory, maintenance, kitchen, ...)
- Extracts structured data with AI agents
- Routes work to **plugins** (bounded operational systems)
- Persists, syncs to Sheets, fans out notifications, surfaces overdue items
- Serves a REST/RPC API consumed by `ops-dashboard`, `visitor-web`, and `field-app`

Keep in mind: solutions built here must stay cheap to run (cost), simple to operate (literacy), and light on bandwidth/compute (device + infrastructure constraints). See root `CLAUDE.md` for the full constraint list.

Stack: **TypeScript + Mastra + Bun + Postgres + Drizzle + Pinecone**. Node `>=22.13.0`.

## Architectural vision (must read)

This subrepo has a canonical architecture doc — **read `ARCHITECTURE.md` before any structural change**. Summary:

- `src/mastra/core/` — channel-agnostic primitives (routing, scheduling, contracts). No WhatsApp/Telegram/visitor specifics here.
- `src/mastra/channels/` — thin adapters (telegram/, whatsapp/). Translate platform events to internal messages. No business logic.
- `src/mastra/domains/` — AI classification + extraction schemas. Not a dumping ground for general logic.
- `src/mastra/plugins/` — bounded operational systems (visitor, inventory, ...). Each owns its rules, routes, jobs, internal helpers. Expose a clean entrypoint; other code depends on that, not deep internals.
- `src/mastra/agents/`, `workflows/`, `tools/`, `scorers/` — Mastra primitives. **Register new ones in `src/mastra/index.ts`.**

The non-negotiable: **a new operational area (e.g. "kitchen orders") should be a new plugin + maybe a new domain, with minimal core changes.**

## Build for the automation surface (consider, don't enforce)

Long-term vision: every domain exposes its **triggers, actions, and events** to the automation engine via `src/mastra/modules/<module>/flow-pack.ts` (see `FLOW_PACK_GUIDE.md`). `visitor`, `cafeteria`, `system` already do this; `inventory` does not yet — and that is fine for now.

You are **not** required to ship a flow-pack with every new feature. You **are** required to leave it cheap to add later:

- At meaningful state transitions (e.g. lot received, low stock, visitor checked in, request approved), have a single clear emit point with a typed payload — not logic smeared across handlers. The eventual `runFlowRules("module", "module.event", payload)` call should be a one-line drop-in.
- Side-effects that a user might plausibly automate (send email, create task, notify channel, create purchase request) should be reachable as **a single function with a typed param object**, so wrapping it as a registered action later is mechanical.
- Use stable, dotted naming for internal events even before they're registered: `inventory.low_stock`, `visitor.checked_in`. That way the eventual trigger key is already chosen.
- If a feature's shape would make later automation-surfacing painful (tightly-coupled side effects, untyped payloads, ad-hoc event names), call it out — don't silently fix it, but don't paint the code into a corner either.

Do **not** add `registerTriggers` / `registerActions` calls or create a `flow-pack.ts` unless the user asks.

## Commands

```bash
npm run dev              # Mastra Studio at localhost:4111 (long-running, separate terminal)
npm run start:backend    # Run the bun HTTP server (port 4112)
npm run bot:dev          # Same with --watch
npm run build            # Production build
npm run dev:all          # Engine + dashboard + visitor-web concurrently
```

Tunnels (for exposing local to WhatsApp webhooks etc.):

```bash
npm run backendtunnel    # ngrok the backend (4112)
npm run visitortunnel    # ngrok the visitor PWA (5174)
npm run mastratunnel     # SSH-tunnel Mastra Studio
```

## Mastra-specific guardrails

- **Before any Mastra-related code change, load the `mastra` skill.** Mastra's APIs change between minor versions; cached knowledge is unreliable. The skill reads up-to-date docs from `node_modules`.
- Use Zod (or equivalent) schemas for every tool's input/output.
- Run `npm run build` to verify compilation after non-trivial changes.
- Don't modify `node_modules` or Mastra's internal DB files.

See `AGENTS.md` for the longer version of these rules.

## Deploy — read this before running `bifrost deploy`

This service deploys to Bifrost project **`auxilio-v2`**, env `dev`, service `ops-engine`. Public URL: `https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site`.

**Always pass `--commit <full-sha>` explicitly.** Bifrost tags images as `<project-id>/<branch>:sha-<short>` at the **project** level. When two services share branch `main`, deploying one without `--commit` can pick up the other service's last image tag. Symptom we hit: the ops-dashboard URL served the visitor-web HTML. Sanity-check after deploy:

```bash
curl -s https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site/health
```

Bifrost is wired to the `personal` remote (`junaid-appointy/office-ops-engine`), not `origin` (`AppointyIncubator/*`). Push to `personal main` before deploying.

Env is loaded from `office-ops-engine/env.updated` (not committed).

## Never do

- Commit `.env`, `env.updated`, or any `*.json` service-account credential files (e.g. `appointy-ops-*.json`, `auxilio-*.json` — already at repo root, treat as secrets).
- Hardcode API keys — always read from env.
- Put visitor-only / inventory-only logic in `core/` or `channels/`.
- Cross-import deep internal files of one plugin from another — go through the public entrypoint.
- Deploy without `--commit`.

## Pointers

- `ARCHITECTURE.md` — canonical architecture & boundaries
- `AGENTS.md` — Mastra rules
- `FLOW_PACK_GUIDE.md` — module contract for exposing triggers/actions/events to the automation engine (read before adding a flow-pack)
- `Generalize_Flow_Editor.md` — flow-editor design notes
- `spec/`, `postman/` — API contracts and request collections
- `drizzle.config.ts`, `data/` — schema and seed data

content.text: Type-check passes. Let me verify the final RosterRow reads cleanly:

Done. Here's what changed and why.

## What I changed

**`features/visit/RosterRow.tsx`** — the per-guest card:
- **Sent guests are now editable.** The gate went from `editable = guest.include && !sent` to `editable = guest.include && !cancelled`. When a host reopens an event for corrections, they can fix a name or phone on a guest whose pass already went out.
- **Details are collapsed by default.** Instead of always-open input boxes, each editable card shows a compact one-line summary: the phone (if set) plus a small inline **"Edit details"** / **"Add phone"** affordance (Lucide `Pencil`). The name/email already live in the head, so a card is now ~one line tall until you choose to open it.
- **Expand on demand.** Clicking the affordance reveals the same name + phone fields, with a **"Done"** button (Lucide `Check`) to collapse again. Edits still persist on blur exactly as before — Done just folds the card back up.
- **Honest feedback for sent guests:** when editing an already-sent guest, a muted line reads *"Re-send to update the pass that's already been issued."* — because corrections only reach the issued pass via the existing **"Review & update passes"** flow.

**`design/global.css`** — added `.guest__summary`, `.guest__phone`, `.guest__edit`, `.guest__done`, indented to align under the name, using existing MD3 tokens (no new colors/magic numbers, no glyphs).

**`package.json`** — bumped `0.1.1` → `0.2.0` (minor, per the extension's "bump on every shipped change" rule).

## Why no engine change was needed
I verified `office-ops-engine`'s `patchDraft` (`visit-drafts.ts:674`) applies `name`/`phone` to **any** guest regardless of status — there was never a server-side block on editing sent guests, only the UI's `!sent` gate. So this stayed a pure presentation change, consistent with the extension's "dumb adapter, engine owns the contract" rule.

`npm run compile` passes. I haven't loaded/reloaded it in Chrome — if you want to eyeball it, `npm run dev` and reload the unpacked build (check the side panel shows v0.2.0).

One thing worth a sanity check on your side: re-sending to an already-sent guest goes through the `materialized` "update passes" path — if you want, I can trace that path to confirm a name/phone correction actually re-issues/updates the live pass rather than no-op'ing, since that's the part that makes editing a sent guest meaningful end-to-end.

content.text:
Built — `.output/chrome-mv3` now has the new bundle, and `manifest.json` shows **0.2.0**.

To see it:
1. Go to `chrome://extensions`
2. Click **Reload** (↻) on the Auxilio extension
3. Close and reopen the side panel

Confirm the version chip reads **0.2.0** — that's the check that the new bundle actually loaded (per the extension's versioning note). If it still shows 0.1.1, the old bundle is cached; reload again there.

A note on workflow: a one-off `npm run build` won't pick up future edits. For iterative work, run `npm run dev` in a terminal — WXT then rebuilds `.output/chrome-mv3` on every save (you still hit Reload in `chrome://extensions` after each change).

===
# Auxilio Workspace

Google **Workspace Add-on** for Auxilio (Office Ops). Lets a host register an in-person visit **from inside the Google Calendar event** — the Google-sanctioned, mobile-capable version of the "Visit tab" idea. This is a **channel surface**, not a backend: it renders cards and forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.

> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules. They apply here too.

## What this is (and isn't)

- **Is:** an Apps Script add-on (CardService) in the Calendar right rail on **web + the mobile Calendar app**. **The whole host flow lives in the card** (card-first, D5) — roster, per-guest include toggle, name/phone edits, template + location pickers, email preview, send, manage — via card form inputs + action handlers that call the engine over HTTPS.
- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, extraction, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle flows all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo is a **dumb adapter** (per the umbrella architecture).
- **Isn't (v1):** a web app. We **do not** bounce the host to a browser tab — that breaks "everything in front of the user," worst on mobile. A full web editor is **deferred/optional** (D6), built only on evidence the card ceiling is real.

## Hard rules (decided 2026-06-16)

1. **Contract lives in the engine, not here.** The add-on POSTs to `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/register` with a Google ID token; the engine verifies it, normalizes to a `NormalizedCalendarEvent{ source: 'marketplace' }`, and feeds the existing spine `ingestCalendarEvent` — the same one the `.ics`/magic-address ingest calls. Dedup is on `tenantId + iCalUID` (the `calendar_event_links` ledger), so the add-on path and the room-resource path converge instead of duplicating. **Send the canonical `iCalUID`** (from the Advanced Calendar Service `Calendar.Events.get(...).iCalUID`) so convergence actually works. Full contract: `../office-ops-engine/spec/2026-06-16_calendar_addon_register_endpoint.md`.
2. **Internal-first.** Published privately to the Appointy Workspace domain (admin one-click push to all users — no per-user install). **No Google OAuth verification needed while internal.** Public Marketplace listing is deferred packaging over this same code.
3. **Two-way without broad OAuth.** Use the narrow current-event scopes:
   - `addOns.calendar.currentEventAccess = READ_WRITE`
   - `.../auth/calendar.addons.current.event.read`, `.../auth/calendar.addons.current.event.write`

   Attendees arrive in the trigger payload (no Calendar API call). `READ_WRITE` lets us write the pass link / "✓ Registered" back onto the *open* event. Broad calendar OAuth stays in the engine's ingest pipeline — **do not duplicate it here.**
4. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail or Drive.** Calendar is a *sensitive* scope (verification but no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → would trigger the annual CASA security assessment. This is a hard line.

## Triggers (manifest)

- `calendar.eventOpenTrigger` → first render of the visitor card (attendees from payload, no backend call).
- `calendar.eventUpdateTrigger` → re-render on attendee add/remove; surface the per-guest include toggle (the online/offline solution).
- action handlers (edit/send) → `UrlFetchApp` POST to `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*` → re-rendered card; then write confirmation back to the event.
- `calendar.homepageTrigger` (Phase 3, optional) → standalone "today's visitors" panel.

## Stack & deploy

- **Runtime:** Google Apps Script (CardService). Google-hosted — **₹0 infra.**
- **Deploy:** `clasp` (Google Apps Script CLI) → Google. **Not Bifrost, not EAS.** The umbrella's "push to `personal` remote for Bifrost deploys" rule does **not** apply here.
- **Auth to engine:** the action handler sends the host's identity (ID token); the engine verifies it so a host can only register visits for their own tenant.

## Status

**Phase 1 MVP card exists** (card-first): `appsscript.json` (manifest) + `Code.js` (CardService card — roster with include toggles + phone, preview, save & send; calls the engine with `getIdentityToken()`). Engine Phase 0 is implemented + tested. To install/test locally see **`SETUP.md`** (ngrok tunnel → local engine, real OAuth token).

Canonical plan: `../Planning-docs/2026-06-16_calendar_visitor_addon_build_plan.md`. Engine contract: `../office-ops-engine/spec/2026-06-16_calendar_addon_register_endpoint.md`. First version of `Code.js` was written outside Google — refine in the Apps Script editor (it can run/log there).

content.text:
Build is done (v0.3.0). Here's everything.

## 1 & 2 — Shipped

**The "scroll" drawer.** Each guest's edit UI is now a subtle, full-bleed strip pinned to the card's bottom edge. Collapsed by default, so a card is just avatar + name + toggle. Tap the strip and the fields unroll downward; the chevron flips.

```
COLLAPSED (default — compact)              EXPANDED (pulled open)
┌────────────────────────────────┐         ┌────────────────────────────────┐
│ (JD) Jane Doe     [Pass sent] ◉ │         │ (JD) Jane Doe     [Pass sent] ◉ │
│      jane@acme.com              │         │      jane@acme.com              │
│════════════════════════════════│         │════════════════════════════════│
│        ⌄  Edit details         │ ←tinted │        ⌃  Hide details         │
└────────────────────────────────┘  strip  │  Visitor name                  │
                                            │  [ 👤 Jane Doe            ]    │
   • edge-to-edge (breaks card padding)     │  Phone (for WhatsApp pass)     │
   • centered label + chevron               │  [ 📞 +91…                ]    │
   • surface-low tint, clipped to corners   │  Re-send to push this change…  │
                                            └────────────────────────────────┘
```

The strip breaks out of the roster Card's 16px padding (negative margins) and I added `overflow: hidden` to that Card so the tint clips to the rounded corners cleanly.

**Sent guests are now toggleable.** Removed the `disabled={sent}` lock. Toggling a sent guest off shows a red **"Will cancel"** chip and dims the row; the next **"Review & update passes"** runs the engine's `applyDraft`, which cancels the pass and notifies the visitor (`visit-drafts.ts:860`) — exactly the add-on's behavior. Toggling back on re-issues. Zero engine changes; the contract already supported it.

Reload `chrome://extensions` (↻) and reopen the side panel — confirm it reads **0.3.0**.

## 3 — Feature gap vs. the add-on

I read all 953 lines of `Code.js`. **At parity or ahead:** include toggle, name/phone edits, template picker, email preview (the extension's real iframe render beats the add-on's popup), send/update/manage mode, sign-in/out, save-event-first, empty/home state. The extension also has a **"Where visitors check in" location field** the add-on lacks.

Genuine gaps, ranked:

| # | Missing in extension | Severity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Calendar auto-sync "Connect calendar" CTA** | **High** | `DraftResponse.calendarSync` is fetched but *never rendered*. The add-on (`addCalendarSyncSection_`) shows a "Connect calendar" button when `relevant && !connected`, so moving/cancelling the event updates passes even with the panel closed. Right now an extension-only host never gets prompted to connect → no background sync. |
| 2 | **Roster stat line** | Medium | Add-on shows `3 guests · 2 sent · 1 pending · 1 cancelled` (color-coded). Extension shows only `Guests · N getting a pass`. Cheap to add, good at-a-glance state. |
| 3 | **Internal-colleague visual** | Low | Add-on: building icon + "Internal · Workspace" overline. Extension: plain "Internal" chip. Cosmetic. |
| 4 | **People API name resolution** | Low | Add-on backfills auto-derived names from Google Contacts. Extension trusts Calendar attendee names. Arguably engine's job; only matters if Calendar names are weak. |
| 5 | **Review sheet doesn't restate cancellations** | Low | Add-on's confirm card lists "Skipped / cancelled if sent before." The new "Will cancel" roster chips cover most of this, but the preview sheet only says "N passes will be sent" — it doesn't surface the count being cancelled. |

My recommendation: **#1 is the one that actually changes outcomes** (silent loss of auto-sync), and **#2** is a quick, high-value polish. #3–5 are cosmetic.

Want me to wire up the Connect-calendar CTA (#1) and the stat line (#2) next? I'd put the CTA in the event-summary card and reuse the engine's existing `connectUrl` — no engine changes.

aiTitle: Add guest detail editing in auxilio-extension

content.text:
Good push — and you're right to question it. I checked the actual sign-in (`lib/auth.ts`), and the permission you take at sign-in is **not** the one auto-sync needs. They're different grants:

**What sign-in gives you (`lib/auth.ts:12-17, 60`):**
- Scope: `calendar.events.readonly` — read-only.
- `response_type=token id_token` — the **implicit** flow. That returns a ~1h **access token with no refresh token**, held in `chrome.storage.session`, silently re-minted via `prompt=none` *only while the user still has a live Google session in the browser*.
- That token **never leaves the extension** — the background worker uses it to read the open event's canonical iCalUID/attendees. The engine only ever receives the **id_token** (`engine.ts` sends just `Authorization: Bearer <idToken>`).

So that grant means: *"the extension can read the event live, while the panel is open."*

**What background auto-sync needs:**
- The **engine** must react to a meeting being moved/cancelled **when nobody has the panel open**. That requires the engine to hold a **persistent offline grant (refresh token) + a Calendar watch channel** on that user's calendar — an authorization-code flow with `access_type=offline`. That's exactly what `calendarSync.connectUrl` kicks off.
- An implicit, read-only, browser-held token *cannot* do this — it can't be used offline, it's never handed to the engine, and it dies with the Google session.

So they don't overlap. Concretely, the only case the connect grant covers that sign-in doesn't is: **meeting silently moves or is cancelled, and no one reopens the extension.** When the panel *is* reopened, `loadDraft` already reconciles attendees/times — so the open-panel case is handled. The gap is purely the passive, closed-panel path, where a stale or zombie pass would otherwise survive.

**Two honest caveats that soften this:**

1. The CTA is gated by the engine's own `calendarSync.relevant` flag (the add-on only renders it when `relevant && !connected`). If the magic-address ingest pipeline already covers that event server-side, the engine returns `relevant: false` and nothing shows. So surfacing it is low-risk — it only appears when the engine says it genuinely adds coverage.

2. There's an alternative to a separate CTA: switch the extension's sign-in itself to an auth-code/offline flow that hands the engine calendar access. But that **duplicates broad calendar OAuth in the extension**, which the add-on's hard rules explicitly forbid ("Broad calendar OAuth stays in the engine's ingest pipeline — do not duplicate it here"). Reusing the engine's existing `connectUrl` keeps that boundary intact.

**Net:** the CTA isn't redundant with sign-in — but whether it's worth showing comes down to one product call: *do you care about move/cancel syncing when the panel is closed?* If yes, surface the engine's existing `connectUrl` (≈free, and self-hiding via `relevant`). If you decide the on-open reconciliation is good enough for extension users, then dropping gap #1 is a legitimate choice — just make it deliberately, not by assuming sign-in already covered it. Want me to wire the self-hiding CTA, or park it?

message.content: yes, I do care for move cancel syncing when panel is closed, but I do not want to make the user sign in twice in the extension.

content.text:
Right — and the good news is you don't have to. "Sign in" and "Connect calendar" are two consent screens only because they're currently two *different OAuth requests*. Merge them into one request and it's one click. The fix is the **flow**, not a second prompt.

## Why it's two prompts today

Your sign-in (`lib/auth.ts`) uses the **implicit** flow:
```
response_type = token id_token      → ephemeral, client-held, no refresh token
scope         = …calendar.events.readonly
```
That can never give the engine offline access, so the engine needs its own separate `connectUrl` consent. Two requests → two screens.

## The single-consent fix

Switch sign-in to the **auth-code flow with offline access**, and let the **engine** exchange the code:

```
response_type = code
access_type   = offline
prompt        = consent              (forces a refresh token on first grant)
scope         = openid email profile calendar.events.readonly   ← unchanged
```

Google shows **one** consent screen for that single request. The engine exchanges the code (it holds the client secret), stores the refresh token, and registers the Calendar watch channel — the same machinery `connectUrl` already drives. After that, `calendarSync.connected` is `true`, so the CTA never appears. Same scope as today, still Calendar-only → **no CASA**.

**Cleanest wiring** (secret never touches the client, and the extension barely changes):

1. `launchWebAuthFlow` points at Google with `redirect_uri = <engine>/oauth/callback`, `state = chrome.identity.getRedirectURL()`.
2. Google → engine callback with `code`. Engine exchanges it → stores refresh token, sets up watch.
3. Engine `302` back to `chrome.identity.getRedirectURL()#id_token=…&access_token=…&expires_in=…`.
4. `launchWebAuthFlow` resolves on that fragment — the extension parses it exactly like it parses the implicit fragment today. The client still gets its id_token + live-read access token; the engine got the offline grant; the extension never even sees the code.

## The honest costs

1. **This needs an engine change** — a `/oauth/callback` (or extending the existing connect backend) to accept the extension's code, exchange it, store the refresh token, and start the watch. That crosses the extension's hard rule *"Engine owns the contract — do not add engine signature/scope changes from here."* You're now prompting it, so it's in scope, but it's **coordinated extension + engine work**, not an extension-only patch.
2. **Consent text gets slightly heavier** — it'll mention access "while you're not using the app." Still one screen, one click.
3. **Config** — the chrome-extension redirect URI on the Web OAuth client, and the client secret living in the engine (which is correct — server-side).
4. **Silent renew** — with `prompt=consent` you can't silently re-mint the *client's* token the old `prompt=none` way on the very first grant; minor plumbing (either keep a parallel silent implicit renew for the client token, or have the engine vend fresh access tokens off its refresh token).

The big unknown that decides effort: **does the engine's existing `connectUrl` backend already store refresh tokens + register the watch in a reusable shape**, so the new callback just feeds the extension's code into it — or is that net-new? That's the difference between a small wiring job and a real piece of engine work.

Want me to dig into the engine's current connect/OAuth backend to answer that, then write a cross-repo implementation plan (this spans `auxilio-extension` + `office-ops-engine` and touches OAuth/secrets, so I'd rather plan it than start hacking)? Or if you'd prefer, I can scope it as exactly what changes in each repo first.

aiTitle: Add guest detail editing in auxilio-extension

content.text: Now the connect start/callback routes themselves:

Dug through the whole connect/sync backend. The headline: **almost everything you need already exists** — the only genuinely missing piece is getting a refresh token into the store during the extension's sign-in. Here's the map.

## What the engine already has (all reusable)

| Piece | Where | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Refresh-token store | `calendar_connections` + `upsertCalendarConnection({email, refreshToken})` | keyed by `host_email` |
| Code→refresh-token exchange | `exchangeCalendarCode(code)` | bound to the engine's `/connect/callback` redirect + the engine's `GOOGLE_OAUTH_*` client |
| Auth URL builder | `buildCalendarAuthUrl` | already `access_type=offline` + `prompt=consent` |
| Connect routes | `GET /connect/start` → Google → `GET /connect/callback` | callback exchanges, upserts, registers watch, shows an HTML "close this tab" page |
| **Closed-panel sync** | `startCalendarSyncLoop()` — `calendar-sync.ts` | **5-min background poll** over every connected host; incremental `syncToken`; feeds `ingestCalendarEvent`. **Poll-based by design** (cost/infra); push/watch is optional |
| `calendarSync` flag | `addon/draft` response | `{connected, canConnect, relevant: isOrganizer, connectUrl}` — the extension already receives this |

The decisive find: **once a refresh token exists in `calendar_connections`, closed-panel move/cancel sync is automatic** — the poll loop picks it up every 5 min, for *all* the host's organized events, not just the open one. No webhooks required. So the problem fully reduces to: **mint a refresh token at sign-in.**

## The one mismatch blocking "one sign-in"

Today's two consents exist because they're two different OAuth *requests*:

- **Extension sign-in**: implicit (`response_type=token id_token`), scope `calendar.events.readonly`, the extension's web client → ephemeral, no refresh token.
- **Engine connect**: code + offline, scope `calendar.events` (read+**write**), the engine's client → refresh token.

One consent screen = one request = one client + one scope set. So they must merge.

## Recommended mechanism — hybrid flow, one screen

Change the extension's interactive sign-in to Google's **hybrid** response:

```
response_type = code token id_token      (was: token id_token)
access_type   = offline
prompt        = consent
scope         = openid email profile calendar.events.readonly   ← unchanged, stays readonly
```

One consent screen returns **both**: the `access_token`+`id_token` in the fragment (extension caches them exactly as `parseFragment` does today — zero change to live reads or silent `prompt=none` renewal) **and** a `code`. The extension then fires the `code` to a new engine endpoint, authed with the Bearer id_token it just got; the engine exchanges it for the **refresh token** and upserts the connection. Done — one click, engine has offline access, the connect CTA auto-hides (and stays as a fallback for anyone who signed in before this change).

Readonly is deliberate: the poll and watch only need read, so we don't widen consent to the engine's read-write scope.

## What actually changes (small, but cross-repo + security-sensitive)

**Extension** (`lib/auth.ts`): add `code` to the response type + `access_type=offline`; capture `code` from the hybrid response; POST it to the engine after caching tokens. Silent renewal untouched.

**Engine**: one new route `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/connect-calendar` — Bearer-authed like the other addon routes, takes `{code, redirectUri}`, exchanges it for the refresh token, `upsertCalendarConnection(...)`, `ensureWatchForHost(...)`. Plus a small `exchangeCalendarCode` variant bound to the **extension's** client + the chrome redirect URI.

**Config / the real gotchas:**
1. **Client secret.** Google **Web** OAuth clients require a secret for code exchange (PKCE alone won't waive it). So the engine must hold the extension client's secret (`AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET`) — good posture, secret stays server-side. The alternative is switching the extension to a "Chrome Extension" client type (PKCE, no secret), which is a bigger config change.
2. **Redirect URI.** `chrome.identity.getRedirectURL()` must be registered as an authorized redirect on that OAuth client, and passed at exchange time.
3. **`scope_version`.** `upsertCalendarConnection` currently hardcodes `CURRENT_SCOPE_VERSION` (= write-capable `v3-events-rw`). A readonly extension grant must be marked distinctly (e.g. `v-ext-ro`) so `isWriteCapableConnection` correctly returns false — otherwise the Slack create-event path would think it can write through an extension token.
4. **Audience.** id_token audience stays the extension's client (already on the `AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` allow-list) — no change there.

This crosses the extension's "don't change the engine contract from here" rule — but you're prompting it, and it's one additive route, not a signature change to the existing ones.

Given it's OAuth, spans two repos, and has those four gotchas, I'd rather **write this up as a proper implementation plan** (hand-off artifact) than start editing. Want me to run the planning skill and produce it? Or if you'd prefer, I can first verify the one open question that most affects feasibility — whether the extension is registered as a **Web** or **Chrome Extension** OAuth client type (decides the secret-vs-PKCE branch).

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/.claude/skills/implementation-planning

# Implementation Planning (for low-effort hand-off)

## Why this exists

A high-effort model reasons through ambiguity, catches edge cases, makes
architectural judgment calls, and keeps cross-file changes consistent. A
low-effort model does much less of that — when it hits a gap, it fills it with a
plausible guess and moves on. That guess is where bugs and rework come from.

The fix is not to make the *implementer* smarter. It's to leave **nothing left to
decide**. Every choice that would otherwise require fresh reasoning during the
build must already be resolved, in writing, in the plan. Done right, the
implementer is transcribing a spec, not solving a problem — and that is something
a low-effort run does reliably.

So the test for every line of the plan is: **could a careful but unimaginative
implementer follow this without making a single judgment call?** If a step
requires the implementer to decide, infer, choose, or "figure out," it is not
done — push that decision up into the plan.

## What to do first

Before writing the plan, do the expensive thinking now (this skill runs at high
effort for exactly this reason):

1. **Read the actual code.** Open the files you'll touch. Don't plan against an
   imagined codebase — plan against the real one, with real names, real types,
   real signatures.
2. **Separate the two kinds of fork.** Some choices are yours to make (which
   pattern, how to structure a function); make them and commit. Others are not
   yours — they depend on product intent, external systems, or acceptance
   criteria only the user knows. Do **not** guess these; collect them as **Open
   questions** and resolve them with the user before the plan is final. A wrong
   requirement guess is the single most expensive kind, because the implementer
   will build it faithfully.
3. **Hunt edge cases up front.** Empty inputs, nulls, concurrency, failure
   paths, large inputs, auth/permissions, partial state. Decide the handling for
   each *now*, because the implementer won't go looking.
4. **Surface invariants.** Anything that must stay true (a contract, an ordering,
   a data shape other code depends on). State it explicitly so the implementer
   doesn't break it by accident.

## Plan structure

ALWAYS produce the plan in this exact shape. Save it as a markdown file
(e.g. `PLAN.md`) so it persists in context during the build. If the plan is long
or the build spans several sessions, tell the implementer to re-read this file at
the start of each session so it never works from a faded memory of it.

```markdown
# Plan: [task name]

## Goal
[2-3 sentences: what we're building and what "done" looks like. Concrete and testable.]

## Open questions (resolve before building)
[Anything that depends on the user's intent or external facts you couldn't settle
yourself. If this section is non-empty, the plan is NOT ready to hand off —
get answers first, then fold them into Decisions and delete them here.]

## Decisions already made
[Every resolved choice — library, pattern, data shape, naming. Each line removes
one judgment call from the implementer.]

## Invariants (do not break)
[Things that must stay true. The implementer should treat these as hard constraints.]

## Ground rules for the implementer
- Follow the steps in order. Do exactly what's specified — no more.
- If you hit anything this plan does not explicitly cover — an undecided choice,
  an unexpected error, or a mismatch between the plan and the actual code — STOP
  and ask rather than guessing. A question is cheaper than a wrong guess built
  faithfully.
- Don't add features, abstractions, or "improvements" beyond the plan (see Out of scope).

## Files to change
[The map: for each file, exact path + one line on what changes. The Steps below
are the actions; this is just so the implementer sees the whole footprint first.]

## Steps
[Numbered, ordered, independently verifiable. For larger work, group steps under
### Phase headings, where each phase ends in a working, verifiable state. See
"Step format" below.]

## Definition of done
[The whole-plan acceptance check: the concrete commands/tests that must pass and
behaviors that must hold for the task to be complete. The implementer runs this
last to confirm the work is actually finished, not just that each step ran.]

## Out of scope
[Explicitly list what NOT to do, so the implementer doesn't gold-plate.]
```

## Step format

Each step is the unit of work the implementer executes. A step is ready only
when it is **literal, isolated, and verifiable**:

- **Literal** — names the exact file, the exact function/class, and (where it
  matters) the exact signature, type, or return shape. Prefer "add
  `parseConfig(raw: string): Config` to `src/config.ts`" over "add config
  parsing." If a snippet removes ambiguity, include the snippet. Anchor edits to
  stable landmarks (a function name, a specific call, a unique string) rather
  than line numbers — line numbers go stale the moment the file is touched.
- **Isolated** — completable on its own and ordered so dependencies come first.
  No step should require information that only appears in a later step.
- **Verifiable** — ends with how to confirm it worked: a command to run, a test
  to add and pass, expected output, or what to check by hand. The implementer
  should never have to *decide* whether a step succeeded.

**Example of a weak step (forces reasoning):**
> 3. Handle errors in the upload flow.

**Example of a ready step (no reasoning needed):**
> 3. In `src/upload.ts`, find the `await s3.put(...)` call inside `uploadFile()`
>    and wrap it in try/catch. On error, log `upload.failed` with the object key
>    and return `{ ok: false, error: 'upload_failed' }` — do **not** throw,
>    because the caller `handleRequest()` in `handler.ts` already branches on
>    `result.ok`. Verify: run `npm test upload.test.ts`; the new
>    "rejects on S3 failure" case should pass.

The example is illustrative — apply the same literal/isolated/verifiable bar in
whatever language and stack the project actually uses.

## Edge cases and failure paths

Don't bury these inside prose steps where a low-effort run will skim past them.
Give edge cases their own explicit treatment — either a dedicated step or a
clearly labeled sub-list under the relevant step — each paired with the exact
intended behavior. If an edge case is intentionally ignored, say so in
**Out of scope** so the implementer doesn't "helpfully" handle it and add
surface area.

## Self-check before finishing

Before handing the plan off, reread it as if you were a literal-minded
implementer with no context and no inclination to think hard. For each step ask:

- Is there any place I'd have to guess a name, type, value, or path? → Specify it.
- Is there a decision phrased as a choice ("either… or", "consider", "as
  appropriate")? → Resolve it, or move it to Open questions if it's the user's call.
- Are there unresolved Open questions? → The plan isn't ready; get answers first.
- Could I tell whether each step succeeded without judgment? → Add verification.
- Does the plan as a whole have a Definition of done I can run at the end? → Add it.
- Are the steps in an order where nothing depends on a later step? → Reorder.
- Did I say what *not* to do? → Add it to Out of scope.

If any answer is "no," fix the plan rather than leaving it for the build phase.
That's the entire point: spend the reasoning here, once, at high effort, so the
implementation run never has to.

## After the plan

Tell the user they can now drop to low or medium effort for the build, and that
the plan file should stay in context (or be re-referenced) so the implementer
works from it directly. Note honestly that if implementation surfaces something
the plan genuinely didn't anticipate, the implementer should stop and ask — and
that specific step is worth bumping back to high effort. The plan reduces fresh
reasoning; it doesn't always eliminate it.

---
name: project_extension_single_consent_connect
description: auxilio-extension single sign-in also connects engine calendar for closed-panel sync (planned, not built)
metadata:
  type: project
---

Goal: one Google consent at extension sign-in establishes BOTH the client tokens AND an engine-side
refresh token in `calendar_connections`, so the engine's 5-min poll syncs move/cancel when the side panel
is closed. No second "Connect calendar" prompt. User cares about closed-panel sync but refused a second sign-in.

Key facts (verified in code): extension sign-in is implicit (`token id_token`), readonly scope, Web OAuth
client; engine already has the full connect/poll/watch machinery (`calendar-oauth.ts`, `calendar-sync.ts` poll
every 5 min, `calendar-watch.ts`). Closed-panel sync = "just get a refresh token into calendar_connections."

Locked design (see Planning-docs/2026-06-23_extension_single_consent_calendar_connect_plan.md):
- Interactive `mint` → hybrid `response_type=code token id_token` + `access_type=offline`; silent renew stays
  implicit. Fragment yields client tokens (cached as today) + a `code`.
- Extension POSTs `{code, redirectUri}` (Bearer id_token) to NEW engine route
  `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/connect-calendar`; engine exchanges code with the EXTENSION client
  (new `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET`) → stores refresh token. Best-effort; CTA stays as fallback.
- CRITICAL: a refresh token can only be refreshed by its minting client. Extension token ≠ engine GOOGLE_OAUTH
  client → add `clientForConnection(conn)` selecting client by `scope_version`; use it in poll + watch, else the
  first poll throws invalid_grant and revokes the connection.
- Stays readonly (`EXTENSION_SCOPE_VERSION='v-ext-events-ro'`, excluded from scope-stale nudge, never write-capable);
  don't downgrade an existing rw/Slack connection. Web client kept (secret server-side), no PKCE, no new redirect URI.

Related: [[project_calendar_chrome_extension]] [[project_calendar_visitor_addon]] [[project_calendar_flow_consolidation]]

Goal: one Google consent at extension sign-in establishes BOTH the client tokens AND an engine-side
refresh token in `calendar_connections`, so the engine's 5-min poll syncs move/cancel when the side panel
is closed. No second "Connect calendar" prompt. User cares about closed-panel sync but refused a second sign-in.

Key facts (verified in code): extension sign-in is implicit (`token id_token`), readonly scope, Web OAuth
client; engine already has the full connect/poll/watch machinery (`calendar-oauth.ts`, `calendar-sync.ts` poll
every 5 min, `calendar-watch.ts`). Closed-panel sync = "just get a refresh token into calendar_connections."

Locked design (see Planning-docs/2026-06-23_extension_single_consent_calendar_connect_plan.md):
- Interactive `mint` → hybrid `response_type=code token id_token` + `access_type=offline`; silent renew stays
  implicit. Fragment yields client tokens (cached as today) + a `code`.
- Extension POSTs `{code, redirectUri}` (Bearer id_token) to NEW engine route
  `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/connect-calendar`; engine exchanges code with the EXTENSION client
  (new `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET`) → stores refresh token. Best-effort; CTA stays as fallback.
- CRITICAL: a refresh token can only be refreshed by its minting client. Extension token ≠ engine GOOGLE_OAUTH
  client → add `clientForConnection(conn)` selecting client by `scope_version`; use it in poll + watch, else the
  first poll throws invalid_grant and revokes the connection.
- Stays readonly (`EXTENSION_SCOPE_VERSION='v-ext-events-ro'`, excluded from scope-stale nudge, never write-capable);
  don't downgrade an existing rw/Slack connection. Web client kept (secret server-side), no PKCE, no new redirect URI.

Related: [[project_calendar_chrome_extension]] [[project_calendar_visitor_addon]] [[project_calendar_flow_consolidation]]

content.text:
The plan is written and ready. Here's the summary.

## Plan: `Planning-docs/2026-06-23_extension_single_consent_calendar_connect_plan.md`

**The mechanism in one line:** interactive sign-in switches to Google's **hybrid flow** (`code token id_token` + `access_type=offline`) — one consent returns the client tokens (cached as today) *and* a `code`; the extension hands that code to a new engine route, which exchanges it for a refresh token and stores it in `calendar_connections`. The existing 5-min poll then handles closed-panel move/cancel. No second prompt.

**The non-obvious risk the plan is built around:** a refresh token can only be refreshed by the OAuth client that minted it. The extension's token belongs to the *extension's* Web client, but the engine's poll/watch rebuild their client from `GOOGLE_OAUTH_*`. Left unhandled, the **first poll throws `invalid_grant` → `markConnectionRevoked`**, silently killing the connection. So the plan adds `clientForConnection(conn)` that picks the client by `scope_version`, used in the poll + the three watch sites. This is flagged as the must-not-break path.

**Reuse vs. new:**
- *Reused as-is:* `calendar_connections`, `upsertCalendarConnection`, the poll loop, the watch machinery, the `calendarSync` flag, the addon auth gate (which already lists the extension client id).
- *New:* `exchangeExtensionCalendarCode`, `clientForConnection`, `EXTENSION_SCOPE_VERSION`, one route `POST /addon/connect-calendar`, one config accessor, and the extension-side hybrid `mint` + `engine.connectCalendar`.

**5 phases**, each ending in a `npm run build`/`compile` checkpoint; verbatim code snippets; explicit edge-case table; a runnable Definition of Done (incl. a read-only DB check). Decisions locked: readonly scope kept (least-privilege), no downgrade of an existing rw/Slack connection, best-effort/non-blocking so sign-in never fails on it, Web client + server-side secret (no PKCE, no new redirect URI).

**One config prerequisite** (Phase 5, not code): the engine needs `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET` (the Web client's secret) added to its runtime env, and `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` confirmed equal to `WXT_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID`.

I also recorded the locked design in memory so it survives across sessions.

You can drop to **low/medium effort** for the build — keep this plan file in context and have the implementer work straight from it. The one place worth staying at high effort if it surfaces: if the live OAuth client turns out *not* to already have the chrome redirect registered, or Google declines the hybrid `code` for this Web client, stop and reassess rather than guessing.

message.content: how is the slack oauth and the add-on auth is handling this, as I do not see any problem in those flows and the move and delete feature works even if the slack or add on is not active or open.

content.text:
Good question — it gets at exactly why the extension is the odd one out. Short answer: **Slack and the add-on never have the dual-client problem because their entire OAuth happens through the *engine's* client — one client mints the refresh token and the same client refreshes it.** The extension is different only because it mints tokens under its *own* Web client. Let me trace it.

## Slack and add-on: one client, end to end

Both route the actual calendar grant through the engine's browser redirect flow:

```
Slack/add-on "Connect calendar" button
   → GET /connect/start         (engine)
   → Google consent              (engine's GOOGLE_OAUTH_* client, calendar.events, offline)
   → GET /connect/callback       (engine)
   → exchangeCalendarCode(code)  → refresh token, minted by the ENGINE client
   → stored in calendar_connections
```

Then the background poll refreshes it:

```
calendar-sync.ts → clientForRefreshToken(token) → buildClient() → GOOGLE_OAUTH_* (same engine client) ✓
```

Same `client_id`/`secret` on both ends, so the refresh just works. There's no binding mismatch to handle — which is exactly why you "don't see a problem" in those flows. Today **every** row in `calendar_connections` was minted by the engine client, so a single `buildClient()` covers refresh for all of them.

## Why "move/delete works when Slack/add-on are closed"

Because the sync isn't done by Slack or the card — it's done by the **engine's 5-minute poll** over `calendar_connections`. The card/Slack are just UIs that *kick off* the connection; once the refresh token is stored, the engine syncs that host's calendar forever, independent of whether anything is open. Your observation is the architecture working as intended: lifecycle (move/delete) is powered by the **per-host OAuth connection's poll/watch**, not by the surface being active. (That's the consolidation rule we locked: *lifecycle = OAuth watch on an owned event.*) The add-on's own copy says as much — "Connect your calendar so moving or cancelling updates passes automatically, **even when this panel is closed**." Without that connect step, an add-on host's move/delete would *not* auto-sync; you're seeing it work because the host connected.

## So why is the extension special?

The extension can't use a server-redirect flow for everything, because its **background worker reads the live event client-side** (it holds a Calendar `access_token` and calls the Calendar API directly for `RESOLVE_EVENT`). That client-side capability is why it has its **own** Web OAuth client + `launchWebAuthFlow`. The moment it mints a refresh token under *that* client, the engine's poll — which refreshes with the *engine* client — can't refresh it. That's the entire reason for `clientForConnection` in the plan. It's not a flaw in the extension approach; it's the price of doing live reads in the browser instead of on the server.

## Which means you have a genuine choice

Since one consent screen = one OAuth client, a single-consent extension sign-in must pick **which** client carries both jobs:

| | Plan's way (Option 1) — keep the extension's own client | Mirror Slack/add-on (Option 2) — extension adopts the engine client |
|---|---|---|
| New refresh logic | `clientForConnection(conn)` selects client by `scope_version` (~10 lines + 4 call-site swaps) | **None** — poll/watch already use the engine client |
| OAuth client coupling | Extension stays isolated | Extension's chrome redirect must be added to the **admin-SSO** client |
| Addon auth trust | Unchanged | Must add `GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` to the addon audience → **any admin-SSO id_token could call `/addon/*`** |
| Consent scope | `calendar.events.readonly` (lighter) | `calendar.events` read-**write** (heavier text) |
| Client-side live reads | Unchanged | Now use an engine-client access token (fine, but re-tested) |

Option 2 is "do exactly what Slack/add-on do" and removes the helper you flagged — but it widens what the `/addon/*` routes will trust and couples the extension to the admin-SSO client. Option 1 keeps clean separation; the helper is fully deterministic (not the scary kind of risk — the only failure mode is "forgot to swap a call site," caught at build/first-poll).

**My recommendation stays Option 1** (the plan), mainly to avoid pouring admin-SSO tokens into the addon audience. But this is a legitimate fork, and given you're explicitly pointing at the Slack/add-on flows as the model, if you'd rather the extension be a carbon copy of them — accepting the audience-widening — I'll switch the plan to Option 2. Want me to, or keep it as written?

## Decisions already made

  `response_type=code token id_token` + `access_type=offline` (keeps `prompt=consent select_account`). The

  token can only be exchanged for access tokens by the **same client_id + client_secret** that minted it.

input.new_string:
- **Client-binding invariant (the reason for the per-connection client selector below):** a Google refresh
  token can only be exchanged for access tokens by the **same client_id + client_secret** that minted it.
  The extension token is minted by the extension Web client, so the engine must refresh/watch it with the
  **extension** client — not the `GOOGLE_OAUTH_*` engine client.

## Why the extension needs special handling (vs. Slack / add-on)

The Slack connect and the Calendar add-on have **no** dual-client problem, and their move/delete sync already
works while those surfaces are closed. Understanding why pins down exactly what the extension must replicate.

- **One client, end to end (Slack & add-on).** Both route the actual calendar grant through the engine's browser
  redirect flow: `"Connect calendar" → GET /connect/start → Google consent (engine GOOGLE_OAUTH_* client,
  calendar.events, offline) → GET /connect/callback → exchangeCalendarCode(code) → refresh token, minted by the
  ENGINE client → stored in calendar_connections`. The background poll then refreshes that token with
  `clientForRefreshToken() → buildClient() → the same GOOGLE_OAUTH_* client`. Same `client_id`/`secret` on both
  ends → refresh just works. **Every existing `calendar_connections` row was minted by the engine client**, which
  is why a single `buildClient()` covers all of them today and nothing looks broken.
- **"Works while closed" = engine-side poll, not the surface.** Move/delete sync is done by the engine's 5-min
  poll over `calendar_connections`, *not* by Slack or the card. Those surfaces only kick off the connection; once
  the refresh token is stored, the engine syncs that host forever regardless of what's open. (This is the locked
  rule: *lifecycle = OAuth watch/poll on an owned event* — see `project_calendar_flow_consolidation`. An add-on
  host who never connected does **not** get closed-panel move/delete; the add-on's own copy says "Connect your
  calendar so moving or cancelling updates passes automatically, even when this panel is closed.")
- **Why the extension is the exception.** The extension's background worker reads the live event **client-side**
  (it holds a Calendar `access_token` and calls the Calendar API directly for `RESOLVE_EVENT`). That client-side
  capability is the reason it has its **own** Web OAuth client + `launchWebAuthFlow`, instead of a server redirect.
  The moment it mints a refresh token under that client, the engine's poll (which refreshes with the engine client)
  cannot refresh it → first poll throws `invalid_grant` → `markConnectionRevoked`. `clientForConnection` (D4) is
  the fix and exists **only** because the extension mints under a different client than the engine.

**The fork this creates** — a single consent screen = a single OAuth client, so one client must carry both the
client-side tokens and the engine's refresh token. Two ways to satisfy that:

| | **Option 1 (this plan)** — keep the extension's own Web client | **Option 2** — extension adopts the engine `GOOGLE_OAUTH_*` client (mirror Slack/add-on) |
|---|---|---|
| New refresh logic | `clientForConnection(conn)` picks client by `scope_version` (~10 lines + 4 call-site swaps) | **None** — poll/watch already use the engine client |
| OAuth client coupling | Extension stays isolated | Extension's chrome redirect must be registered on the **admin-SSO** client |
| `/addon/*` auth trust | Unchanged | Must add `GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` to the addon audience → **any admin-SSO id_token could call `/addon/*`** |
| Consent scope | `calendar.events.readonly` (lighter) | `calendar.events` read-**write** (heavier consent text) |
| Client-side live reads | Unchanged | Now use an engine-client access token (works, but must be re-tested) |

**Decision: Option 1**, chosen mainly to avoid pouring admin-SSO tokens into the `/addon/*` audience and to keep
least-privilege scope. `clientForConnection` is fully deterministic — the only failure mode is forgetting a
call-site swap, caught at build / first poll. If you later prefer the extension to be a carbon copy of the
Slack/add-on flow (accepting the audience-widening and rw scope), switch to Option 2: drop steps 6, 9, 10 and the
`scope_version` handling, set `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID = GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID`, point the extension's
`mint` at the engine client with `calendar.events` scope, and have the engine exchange via `exchangeCalendarCode`.

## Open questions (resolve before building)

content.text:
Added. The plan now has a dedicated section — **"Why the extension needs special handling (vs. Slack / add-on)"** — placed right after Background, covering:

1. **One client, end to end** — how Slack/add-on mint *and* refresh their token under the engine's `GOOGLE_OAUTH_*` client (so every existing `calendar_connections` row is engine-client-bound, and a single `buildClient()` covers all of them — which is why nothing looks broken).
2. **"Works while closed" = engine poll, not the surface** — the sync is the 5-min poll over stored tokens, decoupled from whether Slack/the card is open; with the consolidation rule cited.
3. **Why the extension is the exception** — it reads the live event client-side, so it has its own Web client + `launchWebAuthFlow`, which is the sole reason `clientForConnection` (D4) exists.
4. **The Option 1 vs Option 2 fork** — the comparison table, the committed decision (Option 1, to avoid widening the `/addon/*` audience to admin-SSO tokens), and a precise "if you ever want Option 2, here's exactly what to change" escape hatch (drop steps 6/9/10 + scope_version, set `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID = GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID`, point `mint` at the engine client, exchange via `exchangeCalendarCode`).

The decision stays Option 1 as planned. If you'd rather I flip the whole plan to Option 2 (the exact Slack/add-on mirror), say so and I'll rewrite the affected steps — otherwise it's ready to hand off to a low/medium-effort build.
