message.content:
what do you suggest, which is better, reliable, scalable and better engineering?
  Option 2 is "do exactly what Slack/add-on do" and removes the helper you flagged — but it widens what the /addon/* routes will trust and couples the extension to the admin-SSO
  client. Option 1 keeps clean separation; the helper is fully deterministic (not the scary kind of risk — the only failure mode is "forgot to swap a call site," caught at
  build/first-poll).

  My recommendation stays Option 1 (the plan), mainly to avoid pouring admin-SSO tokens into the addon audience. But this is a legitimate fork, and given you're explicitly
  pointing at the Slack/add-on flows as the model, if you'd rather the extension be a carbon copy of them — accepting the audience-widening — I'll switch the plan to Option 2.
  Want me to, or keep it as written?

content.text:
Straight answer: **Option 1.** It's not close once you weigh scalability and security against a roadmap you've already committed to. My earlier "mainly to avoid audience-widening" undersold it — here's the real case across your four axes.

**Scalable — the decisive one.** Your own roadmap says the extension goes **internal-first, then public marketplace** (`project_calendar_chrome_extension`). A public, Google-verified marketplace extension **cannot ride the internal admin-SSO client** — different verification, branding, scopes, consent screen. So Option 2 has a built-in expiry date: the day you publish, you're forced to give the extension its own client, which means you build `clientForConnection` anyway — just later, under more pressure, with live connections to migrate. Option 1 establishes the right shape now: *each surface owns its OAuth client; the engine refreshes per-connection by looking up the minting client.* Adding the next channel (Telegram, partner tenant, public extension) is "new `scope_version` branch," not a re-architecture. Option 2 is a point solution that the known future breaks.

**Reliable.** Roughly a wash on paper — Option 2 reuses the proven poll, Option 1 adds ~10 lines. But Option 2 buys reliability by **coupling**: rotate the dashboard client's secret, change its scopes, or trip its quota, and the extension breaks *with* it, for non-obvious reasons. Separate clients isolate quotas, incidents, and blast radius. Option 1's failure mode ("forgot a call-site swap") is static, enumerable (4 sites), and caught at build/first-poll — the plan's Definition of Done already asserts "no `invalid_grant` for your host in the poll logs," which trips on exactly that. Isolated-and-loud beats coupled-and-quiet.

**Secure.** Option 2 sets `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID = GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID`, so **any admin-SSO id_token can call `/addon/*`** (only domain-gated, not role-gated), and it registers the chrome redirect on the admin-SSO client — so a compromised extension mints admin-SSO-audience tokens. That conflates "admin signed into the dashboard" with "host registering a visit" and enlarges the blast radius. Option 1 keeps those trust domains apart. Clear win.

**Better engineering.** Option 2's coupling is a *config coincidence* (two env vars happening to be equal), not expressed intent — the kind of thing that ambushes a maintainer a year later. Option 1's helper makes a real invariant explicit and enforced in code: *a refresh token can only be refreshed by its minting client.* Making a constraint visible and checked is better than erasing the distinction it rests on. The "scary helper" framing was wrong — it's the opposite; it's the documentation of why multi-surface OAuth works.

One honest forward note: the *most* scalable end-state is the engine owning calendar reads entirely (extension becomes a pure id_token client, no client-side access token at all). That's a bigger refactor for another day — but notice Option 1 moves **toward** it (per-connection client management already lives in the engine) while Option 2 moves **away** (surfaces get entangled). That alignment is the tiebreaker even if everything else were equal.

The plan is already written as Option 1, so nothing to change to proceed. The one improvement I'd make: the doc's rationale currently leads with the audience-widening point — I'd reorder it to lead with the **public-marketplace scalability** argument, since that's the strongest and most concrete. Want me to make that edit?

**Decision: Option 1.** Primary reason is **scalability against the committed roadmap**: the extension is planned
to go internal-first → public marketplace, and a Google-verified public extension *cannot* use the internal
admin-SSO client — so Option 2 has a built-in expiry date, at which point you'd build `clientForConnection`
anyway, later and with live connections to migrate. Option 1 establishes the extensible shape now (each surface
owns its OAuth client; the engine refreshes per-connection), keeps the `/addon/*` trust boundary and least-privilege
scope intact, and avoids coupling the extension to the admin-SSO client's lifecycle. `clientForConnection` is fully
deterministic — the only failure mode is forgetting a call-site swap, caught at build / first poll (the Definition
of Done's "no `invalid_grant` in the poll logs" check trips on exactly this). It is also the direction of the
longer-term end-state (engine owns all calendar reads; extension becomes a pure id_token client): Option 1 moves
toward it, Option 2 moves away.

If you ever do want the extension to be a carbon copy of the Slack/add-on flow (accepting the audience-widening
and rw scope — only sane while the extension stays permanently internal), switch to Option 2: drop steps 6, 9, 10
and the `scope_version` handling, set `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID = GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID`, point the
extension's `mint` at the engine client with `calendar.events` scope, and have the engine exchange via
`exchangeCalendarCode`.

content.text:
Both repos are green. Build complete.

## What shipped (Option 1, per the plan)

**office-ops-engine** (Phases 1–3, builds clean):
- `lib/config.ts` — `auxilioExtensionOauth` accessor (client id + secret).
- `calendar-oauth.ts` — `EXTENSION_SCOPE_VERSION`, `isAuxilioExtensionOAuthConfigured()`, `exchangeExtensionCalendarCode()`, **`clientForConnection()`** (the per-connection client selector), `upsertCalendarConnection` now takes `scopeVersion`, and `listScopeStaleConnections` excludes the extension marker.
- `calendar-sync.ts` + `calendar-watch.ts` — poll and all three watch sites now refresh via `clientForConnection(conn)`, so an extension-minted token is refreshed with the extension client (the must-not-break path). `calendar.ts` write path left on the engine client intentionally.
- `routes.ts` — new `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/connect-calendar` inside the addon auth block: best-effort, always 200, no-downgrade guard for already-connected hosts (D7).

**auxilio-extension** (Phase 4, type-checks + builds, manifest **0.4.0**):
- `lib/engine.ts` — `connectCalendar(idToken, code, redirectUri)`.
- `lib/auth.ts` — interactive `mint` now hybrid (`code token id_token` + `access_type=offline`); silent renew unchanged; `signIn` fires the best-effort connect with the code.
- `.env.example` note + version bump.

## What's left (yours / verification)

1. **Add `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET`** to the engine env (you said you'd do this). `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` is already present.
2. **Deploy the engine** — the code is built locally but not deployed. Per your repo rules I haven't pushed/deployed; when you're ready it's `bifrost deploy ... --commit <sha>` to `ops-engine`. The route + env both need to be live for the flow to work.
3. **Reload the extension** in `chrome://extensions` (confirm it shows 0.4.0).
4. **End-to-end check** (from the plan's Definition of Done, runnable once the secret is set + engine deployed): fresh interactive sign-in → one consent → `calendar_connections` has a `connected` row with `scope_version='v-ext-events-ro'` and a refresh token → `addon/draft` returns `calendarSync.connected: true` → poll log shows your host syncing with no `invalid_grant`.

I didn't run the live route smoke test (it needs the engine running against the dev DB + the secret), and I didn't commit/push/deploy. Want me to stage a commit for the engine + extension changes (separate commits per repo), or leave the working tree as-is for you to review first?

content.text:
Key thing I found first: your local `.env` has `WXT_ENGINE_URL=https://unyearning-olympia-unclimactic.ngrok-free.dev` (the tunnel), and WXT inlines that **at build time** — so the build I made earlier baked **ngrok**, not bifrost. A store build must be built with the bifrost URL. More on that below.

These are two independent axes — don't conflate them:

## 1. Unlisted vs Internal → **Unlisted** (because of your dev account choice)

"Private / Internal" visibility on the Chrome Web Store is **tied to Google Workspace membership** — only publishable from a managed account *inside* the org, and the org admin controls it. Since you're deliberately using a **different (non-org) developer account, Private is not available to you.** So:

- **Publish Unlisted** (not searchable; installable by link/ID), then have the **org admin force-install it by extension ID** via Admin console → Chrome → Apps & Extensions. That gives you the same "internal-only, zero-touch rollout" outcome the CLAUDE anticipated, without needing an org dev account.
- That separate dev account needs its own one-time **$5 CWS registration**, and Unlisted **still goes through CWS review** + needs listing assets (icon, description, **privacy policy URL** — required with calendar scope).

(If you *could* use an org Workspace account as the developer, true Private would be marginally cleaner, but Unlisted + force-install is functionally equivalent and is the standard path here.)

## 2. The OAuth client is separate — keep it in the org, set consent to **Internal**

The CWS publisher account and the Google **OAuth client** are unrelated. Keep the OAuth client (`561888594292-…`) in the **org's GCP project with consent-screen User type = Internal**, regardless of who owns the store listing. This matters for the feature we just built:

- **Internal** = only org-Workspace users can sign in, **no app verification**, and **refresh tokens don't expire**.
- If the consent screen is **External + Testing**, refresh tokens **expire after 7 days** — which would silently break the closed-panel calendar sync we just wired. (This is the "must be In Production for a non-expiring refresh token" note from the plan; Internal sidesteps it entirely.)

Do **not** move the OAuth client to the new developer account's GCP project — that flips it to External and triggers verification + the 7-day expiry.

## 3. Which file → the **zip from `npm run zip`**

Upload a **ZIP of the built `chrome-mv3` output**, not a `.crx` and not the unpacked folder (the store re-signs it):

```bash
npm run zip   # WXT → .output/auxilio-extension-0.4.0-chrome.zip
```

Upload that `.zip`.

## 4. Engine URL → build-time env + deploy the engine first

Three things, in order:

1. **Deploy the engine to bifrost first.** Your `.env` comment says the `/addon/*` routes "are not deployed to the bifrost dev engine" — and that now includes the new `/addon/connect-calendar` route + the `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET`. If you point a published extension at bifrost before deploying, every call 404s. So: deploy `ops-engine` (with `--commit`) and set the secret, *then* build the extension.
2. **Build with the bifrost URL**, not ngrok:
   ```bash
   WXT_ENGINE_URL=https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site npm run zip
   ```
   (or change `.env`). Also set a real `WXT_MAGIC_ADDRESS` — it's currently `findajx@gmail.com`.
3. **Drop the ngrok `host_permission`** from `wxt.config.ts` for the published build — it's dev cruft and extra host permissions draw review scrutiny.

## ⚠️ The gotcha that *will* break sign-in if missed

Sign-in uses `chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow`, whose redirect is `https://<extension-id>.chromiumapp.org/`. **The store assigns a new extension ID on upload** (different from your unpacked-dev ID), so that new redirect URI must be added to the OAuth Web client's **Authorized redirect URIs** — otherwise the published extension fails with `redirect_uri_mismatch`. Either:
- Upload once → copy the assigned ID → add `https://<store-id>.chromiumapp.org/` to the OAuth client, **or**
- Pin a `key` in the manifest so dev and store share one ID (and register that one redirect up front).

---

Want me to prep the production build config now — remove the ngrok host from `wxt.config.ts`, and produce the store zip built against the bifrost URL? I'd hold off until you confirm the engine is deployed there with the secret, since that's the real blocker. And I still won't deploy/commit anything without your go-ahead.

message.content: even in an unlisted form do I need to fill all the details and justification for the permission?

content.text:
Short answer: **yes.** "Unlisted" only changes **discoverability** (not searchable, not shown in categories). It goes through the **exact same review** as a public item, so you still complete the full listing and all the permission/privacy justifications. There's no reduced-paperwork tier for unlisted.

## What you'll have to fill regardless of visibility

- **Listing basics:** name, a description, a 128×128 icon, at least one **screenshot** (1280×800 or 640×400), category, language.
- **Single purpose** statement (one sentence on what the extension does).
- **Permission justifications** — for each permission/host permission the dashboard flags, a short "why it's needed." Yours will all get flagged (`identity`, host permissions, etc.).
- **Data-usage / privacy practices** disclosures + the compliance certification (you don't sell data, etc.).
- **A privacy policy URL** — **required** here, because the extension handles user data (calendar + identity). This is the one people most often get stuck on.

OAuth verification is a *separate* track from CWS review — but since your consent screen will be **Internal**, that side stays clear.

## If the justification/privacy burden is the real concern — there's an escape hatch

For a Workspace org you can **skip the Chrome Web Store entirely**: self-host the packed extension (an HTTPS-served `.crx` + an `update.xml` manifest) and have the org admin **force-install it via the Admin console** (ExtensionInstallForcelist pointing at your update URL). No listing, no screenshots, no permission justifications, no review.

Trade-off vs. unlisted-CWS:
- **Self-host:** zero CWS paperwork, but you host the `.crx`/update manifest yourself, manage updates manually, and it needs org-admin cooperation. The extension ID is also stable (derived from your signing key — which conveniently fixes the redirect-URI problem too).
- **Unlisted CWS:** Google hosts + auto-updates, but you do the full listing + justifications once.

Since you're already planning admin force-install, self-hosting is a legitimate option if you'd rather not deal with the store form at all.

---

Want me to **draft the permission justifications + single-purpose + data-usage answers** for the CWS form? I've read the code, so I can write the exact "why" for each of `identity`, `sidePanel`, `storage`, `alarms`, `notifications`, and the four host permissions — you'd paste them straight in. Or, if you'd rather go the self-host route, I can lay out the `.crx` + `update.xml` + Admin-console steps instead.

aiTitle: Add guest detail editing in auxilio-extension

content.text:
Here's copy-paste-ready content for each field. I've written the permission justifications to match what the code actually does (I've read it), and kept them honest — reviewers reject vague or overbroad justifications.

## Single purpose
```
Register office visitors and issue visitor passes directly from a Google Calendar event.
```

## Detailed description (≥25 chars)
```
Auxilio Visitor lets a meeting host register in-person visitors without leaving Google Calendar. Open a calendar event that includes outside guests, choose who should get a visitor pass, correct names and phone numbers, preview the invitation email, and send — all from the side panel. Passes are issued by your organization's Auxilio backend. This extension is for internal company use.
```

## Permission justifications

**identity**
```
Signs the host in with their Google account via chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow to obtain an OAuth token. The token authenticates the host to our backend (proving who is registering the visit) and authorizes read access to the Google Calendar event the host currently has open. No other use of identity.
```

**Host permission use**
```
calendar.google.com — content script detects which calendar event is open and injects the "Register a visitor" trigger.
www.googleapis.com — reads the open Calendar event (events.get) to obtain its canonical ID, attendees, and start/end times.
people.googleapis.com — resolves guest display names and photos from the host's Google contacts to populate the visitor roster.
ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site — our organization's Auxilio backend API; the extension forwards the host's intent here to build the visitor draft, render the email preview, and issue passes.
```

**notifications**
```
Shows a desktop notification when a new upcoming event that needs visitor passes is detected, reminding the host to register guests. Triggered by the background sync only; never used for marketing.
```

**alarms**
```
Runs a periodic background check (chrome.alarms, roughly once a minute) to refresh the set of upcoming events that need visitor passes and to update the toolbar badge and notification. No other scheduled work.
```

**storage**
```
Stores the host's session/OAuth token and the currently-open event context in chrome.storage.session (cleared when the browser closes) so the side panel and the content script stay in sync. No long-term storage of personal data.
```

**sidePanel**
```
The entire user interface is rendered in the Chrome side panel; clicking the toolbar icon opens it. Required to host the visitor-registration UI next to Google Calendar.
```

**Remote code use**
```
The extension does not load or execute remote code. All scripts are bundled in the package. It fetches JSON from our backend API and renders the invitation-email preview as inert, sandboxed HTML (an iframe with no script execution — sandbox allows same-origin only, not scripts). No eval, no externally hosted scripts.
```

## Data usage (certification tab)
Declare collected data:
- **Personally identifiable information** (name, email of host and guests) — **Yes**, used to issue visitor passes.
- **Authentication information** (OAuth token) — **Yes**.
- Everything else (location, health, financial, web history, personal communications, user activity, website content) — **No**.

Then check all three certifications as **true**:
1. Not selling/transferring data to third parties outside approved use cases.
2. Not using/transferring data for purposes unrelated to the single purpose.
3. Not using/transferring data for creditworthiness or lending.

(Those are all accurate for this extension.)

## Assets you still need to supply
- **Icon (store listing):** the store wants a 128×128 PNG uploaded separately from the manifest. You already have it: `auxilio-extension/public/icon/128.png` — upload that.
- **Screenshot (≥1, 1280×800 or 640×400):** capture the side panel open next to a calendar event. Quickest path: load the extension, open the panel on a test event, screenshot it, then pad/resize to exactly 1280×800. If you grab a raw screenshot and drop it in the folder, I can pad it to the required dimensions for you with a one-liner.
- **Privacy policy URL:** not in your current error list, but once you declare data collection above it will be required (calendar + identity data). It needs to be a public URL stating what you collect (calendar event data, name/email, OAuth token), why (to issue visitor passes), that you don't sell it, and how to contact you. Want me to draft the privacy-policy text too so you can host it?

Want me to draft the privacy policy, and/or pad a screenshot once you capture one?

input.content:
# Privacy Policy — Auxilio Visitor (Chrome Extension)

_Last updated: 26 June 2026_

Auxilio Visitor ("the extension") is an internal tool that lets a meeting host
register in-person office visitors directly from a Google Calendar event. This
policy explains what data the extension accesses, why, and how it is handled.

## Who this is for

The extension is distributed for internal use within our organization. It is not
a general-public consumer product.

## What data we access

- **Your Google account identity** — your email address and name, obtained when
  you sign in, to authenticate you to our Auxilio backend and to authorize
  reading the calendar event you have open.
- **Google Calendar event data (read-only)** — the title, date/time, location,
  and attendee list of the event you are actively viewing, used to build the
  visitor roster. The extension requests only the
  `calendar.events.readonly` scope. It never requests Gmail or Drive access.
- **Visitor details you enter** — guest names and phone numbers you add or
  correct in order to issue a pass.
- **Contact display names/photos** — resolved from your Google contacts (People
  API) to label guests in the roster.
- **Authentication tokens** — short-lived OAuth tokens used to make the above
  requests.

## How we use it

This data is used solely to register visitors and issue visitor passes through
your organization's Auxilio backend. It is not used for advertising, profiling,
or any purpose unrelated to that single function.

## How it is shared

- Event and visitor details are sent to your organization's Auxilio backend
  (operated by us) to create and send visitor passes.
- We do **not** sell or rent your data, and we do **not** share it with third
  parties outside the visitor-pass workflow.

## Storage and retention

- OAuth tokens and the currently-open event context are kept in the browser's
  session storage and are cleared when the browser closes.
- Visitor and event records created when you issue a pass are stored by the
  Auxilio backend for operational and audit purposes, under your organization's
  data-retention practices.

## Your choices

- You can sign out from the extension at any time, which clears the local
  session.
- You can revoke the extension's access to your Google account at
  https://myaccount.google.com/permissions.

## Contact

Questions about this policy: privacy@appointy.com

message.content: okay I want to manually share the extension to a friend, how can I do that?

content.text:
For sharing with one friend (outside the store), **"Load unpacked" is the way** — but there's one gotcha that will silently break sign-in, so let me cover both.

## The method

1. Zip the built output folder and send it:
   ```
   auxilio-extension/.output/chrome-mv3/
   ```
   (Send the `chrome-mv3` *folder* zipped — not the `0.11.5-chrome.zip`, which is structured for store upload, though that also works if they unzip it.)
2. Your friend: `chrome://extensions` → toggle **Developer mode** on → **Load unpacked** → select the unzipped folder.

That loads the UI immediately. **Don't bother with a `.crx`** — Chrome blocks installing `.crx` files dragged in from outside the Web Store on the stable channel, so it won't install for a normal user.

## The gotcha: sign-in won't work as-is

Two things must be true for your friend to actually **sign in** (not just see the UI):

1. **Stable extension ID.** Sign-in uses `chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow`, whose redirect is `https://<extension-id>.chromiumapp.org/`. A load-unpacked build **gets a different ID on each machine** unless you pin a `key` in the manifest — so your friend's install would have an ID whose redirect URI isn't registered on the OAuth client → sign-in fails with `redirect_uri_mismatch`. Fix: pin a `key` (one stable ID for everyone), then register that one `https://<id>.chromiumapp.org/` on the OAuth Web client's Authorized redirect URIs.

2. **Their Google account must be allowed by your OAuth consent screen:**
   - If the consent screen is **Internal** (org Workspace), only accounts in that org can sign in — an outside friend **cannot**, regardless of anything else.
   - If it's **External**, add your friend's email under **Test users** in the Google Cloud consent screen (Testing mode allows up to 100), and they can sign in (with an "unverified app" warning they click through).

Also remember the engine must be deployed at the bifrost URL with the new routes + secret, or every backend call 404s on their machine too.

## Want me to set it up?

I can pin a stable `key` now: generate a keypair, add the public key to `wxt.config.ts`, rebuild, and give you back **(a)** the resulting extension ID and **(b)** the exact `https://<id>.chromiumapp.org/` redirect URI to paste into the OAuth client. Then your friend's install and yours share one ID and sign-in works.

One caveat to decide first: if you later publish to the Web Store, the store assigns its *own* ID unless you reconcile keys — so you'd either register both redirect URIs or align the keys. For just sharing with a friend now, a self-generated pinned key is the quick path.

Want me to pin the key and rebuild?

content.text: On it — pinning a stable key, rebuilding, and producing the share zip. First I'll generate the keypair, derive the public key + extension ID, and check the current config/gitignore.

input.command:
bash -c '
set -e
cd /Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/auxilio-extension
openssl genrsa 2048 > key.pem 2>/dev/null
openssl rsa -in key.pem -pubout -outform DER -out /tmp/auxilio_pub.der 2>/dev/null
PUBKEY=$(openssl base64 -A -in /tmp/auxilio_pub.der)
ID=$(openssl dgst -sha256 -hex /tmp/auxilio_pub.der | sed "s/.*= //" | cut -c1-32 | tr "0-9a-f" "a-p")
echo "PUBKEY=$PUBKEY"
echo "EXTENSION_ID=$ID"
echo "REDIRECT_URI=https://$ID.chromiumapp.org/"
'

1	import { defineConfig } from 'wxt';

3	// Engine host_permission is derived from the build-time engine URL so the
4	// published manifest only ever carries the engine it actually targets (no stale
5	// tunnel hosts). Prod: pass WXT_ENGINE_URL=<bifrost url>. Local dev against the
6	// reserved tunnel: WXT_ENGINE_URL=<ngrok url> npm run dev. Falls back to the
7	// deployed bifrost dev engine when unset.
8	const ENGINE_URL = (
9	  process.env.WXT_ENGINE_URL ?? 'https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site'
10	).replace(/\/$/, '');
11	const ENGINE_HOST = `${new URL(ENGINE_URL).origin}/*`;
12	
13	// See https://wxt.dev/api/config.html
14	// Manifest is intentionally minimal and Calendar-only (no Gmail/Drive — CASA line).
15	// Auth uses chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow (web OAuth client), so no `oauth2`
16	// manifest key is needed; the client id is read from env in lib/config.ts.
17	export default defineConfig({
18	  modules: ['@wxt-dev/module-react'],
19	  manifest: {
20	    name: 'Auxilio Visitor',
21	    description:
22	      'Register office visitors directly from a Google Calendar event.',
23	    permissions: ['identity', 'sidePanel', 'storage', 'alarms', 'notifications'],
24	    host_permissions: [
25	      'https://calendar.google.com/*',
26	      'https://www.googleapis.com/*',
27	      // People API (guest name + photo resolution) lives on its own host.
28	      'https://people.googleapis.com/*',
29	      // Engine — derived from WXT_ENGINE_URL at build time.
30	      ENGINE_HOST,
31	    ],
32	    // Toolbar icon; background flips openPanelOnActionClick so a click opens the panel.
33	    action: {
34	      default_title: 'Auxilio Visitor',
35	      default_icon: {
36	        16: 'icon/16.png',
37	        32: 'icon/32.png',
38	        48: 'icon/48.png',
39	        128: 'icon/128.png',
40	      },
41	    },
42	  },
43	});

content.text:
Done — both the key pinning and the share zip are ready.

## The zip to send
```
/Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/auxilio-extension/auxilio-visitor-share.zip
```
114 KB, clean (no macOS cruft). It unzips to a single `auxilio-visitor/` folder containing `manifest.json` — that's the folder your friend points "Load unpacked" at.

**Friend's steps:** unzip → `chrome://extensions` → enable **Developer mode** → **Load unpacked** → select the `auxilio-visitor` folder.

## Stable identity (now pinned)
- **Extension ID:** `babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn` — same on every machine now, because the public key is pinned in the manifest (verified: the built manifest hashes to exactly this ID).
- **Redirect URI to register** on the OAuth Web client (`561888…`) → Authorized redirect URIs:
  ```
  https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org/
  ```

## You must still do these or sign-in fails for your friend
1. **Add that redirect URI** to the OAuth client in Google Cloud Console (without it: `redirect_uri_mismatch`).
2. **Let your friend's Google account in:** if the consent screen is **Internal**, only your org's accounts can sign in — an outside friend can't. If **External**, add their email under **Test users**.
3. **Engine must be live** at the bifrost URL with the `/addon/*` + `connect-calendar` routes and `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET`, or every backend call 404s.

## Housekeeping / notes
- **`key.pem`** (the private key) was generated in the extension folder and is now **gitignored** (`key.pem`/`*.pem`). Keep it safe — you'll want it if you ever pack a `.crx` or want the same ID. The public key in `wxt.config.ts` is not secret.
- **Store caveat:** if you later publish to the Web Store, it assigns its own ID unless you reconcile keys — you'd then register the store ID's redirect too (or upload using this same key). Not a problem for the friend-share now.
- Your friend will see Chrome's "Disable developer-mode extensions" nag on each launch — that's normal for unpacked installs; they can dismiss it.

Want me to drop the store-listing materials (permission justifications + single purpose + description) into a `STORE_LISTING.md` so they're saved alongside the privacy policy, while you're in this flow?

message.content: so this will be the authorised redirect URL?https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org/

content.text:
Yes — that's the one:

```
https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org/
```

It's exactly what `chrome.identity.getRedirectURL()` returns for this pinned ID (no path argument → `https://<id>.chromiumapp.org/` with the trailing slash), and that's the value the extension sends as `redirect_uri` during sign-in.

Where it goes:
- Google Cloud Console → **APIs & Services → Credentials** → your OAuth client `561888594292-…` (it's a **Web application** type) → **Authorized redirect URIs** → Add.

Two practical tips so you don't lose a round-trip to `redirect_uri_mismatch`:
1. **Exact match matters.** Google compares the redirect URI character-for-character. To be safe against trailing-slash differences across Chrome versions, add **both** forms:
   ```
   https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org/
   https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org
   ```
2. This goes under **Authorized redirect URIs**, *not* "Authorized JavaScript origins."

(One unrelated reminder: the **client secret** of this same Web client still needs to be set on the engine as `AUXILIO_EXTENSION_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET` for the calendar-connect exchange — separate from this redirect URI step.)

content.text: That error reveals the problem: Google sees `redirect_uri=https://**phlplhnahmohnbfdfcemdogompigafjm**.chromiumapp.org/`, but the ID we pinned is **`babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn`**. So the build you loaded into Chrome **doesn't have the pinned key** — Chrome fell back to a path-derived ID (`phlplh…`). You almost certainly loaded the stale dev folder, not the keyed build. Let me confirm which folder is which (local check only):

    HASKEY=$(node -e "const m=require(\"./$d/manifest.json\"); console.log(m.key?\"yes\":\"NO\")")
    if [ "$HASKEY" = "yes" ]; then
      node -e "process.stdout.write(require(\"./$d/manifest.json\").key)" | openssl base64 -d -A > /tmp/k.der 2>/dev/null
      ID=$(openssl dgst -sha256 -hex /tmp/k.der | sed "s/.*= //" | cut -c1-32 | tr "0-9a-f" "a-p")

  fi
done

content.text:
Confirmed the cause:

| Folder | Has pinned key? | Extension ID |
|---|---|---|
| `.output/chrome-mv3` (keyed prod build) | ✅ yes | `babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn` |
| `.output/chrome-mv3-dev` (stale dev build, 23 Jun) | ❌ no | path-derived → **`phlplh…`** ← what you loaded |
| `auxilio-visitor-share.zip` | ✅ yes | `babflij…` |

You loaded **`chrome-mv3-dev`** — the old `npm run dev` folder from before I pinned the key, so Chrome made up a path-based ID (`phlplh…`), which isn't registered.

## Fix — load the keyed build

1. `chrome://extensions` → **remove** the currently-loaded one (the `phlplh…` instance).
2. **Load unpacked** → select:
   ```
   /Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/auxilio-extension/.output/chrome-mv3
   ```
   (or unzip `auxilio-visitor-share.zip` and load the `auxilio-visitor` folder — same thing.)

content.text:
Important distinction first: **you don't upload a folder to the Chrome Web Store — you upload a ZIP whose root is `manifest.json`.** That's the `npm run zip` output (`auxilio-extension-<version>-chrome.zip`), **not** the `auxilio-visitor-share.zip` I made for your friend (that one has a parent `auxilio-visitor/` folder, which is for "Load unpacked" only — the store would reject it).

And there's a key (pun intended) thing to understand about the store vs. the pinned ID — let me build a fresh store zip and then explain.

input.command:
bash -c '
set -e
cd /Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/auxilio-extension
env WXT_ENGINE_URL=https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site \
    WXT_MAGIC_ADDRESS=findajx@gmail.com \
    REDACTED.apps.googleusercontent.com \
    npm run zip 2>&1 | tail -4
echo "=== store zip: structure (manifest must be at ROOT, not nested) ==="
ZIP=$(ls -t .output/*-chrome.zip | head -1)
unzip -l "$ZIP" | sed -n "1,8p"
echo "=== path ==="; ls -lh "$ZIP" | awk "{print \$5, \$9}"
'

content.text:
Fresh store zip is ready, with `manifest.json` at the root (correct store format):

```
/Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/auxilio-extension/.output/auxilio-extension-0.11.5-chrome.zip
```

**Upload that one.** (Not `auxilio-visitor-share.zip` — that's folder-nested, for Load unpacked only.)

## The thing you need to know about the store and the ID

The pinned `babflij…` ID **only applies to load-unpacked installs** (you + your friend). **The Chrome Web Store assigns its own permanent extension ID** to the listing — it ignores the manifest `key` for that purpose. So:

- **Store-installed users** get the store's **Item ID** (shown in the CWS dashboard for your item).
- Their sign-in redirect will be `https://<store-item-id>.chromiumapp.org/` — a **different** URI than `babflij…`.

So you'll end up registering **two** redirect URIs on the OAuth client:
1. `https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org/` — your/friend's load-unpacked builds.
2. `https://<store-item-id>.chromiumapp.org/` — store-installed users. **Get `<store-item-id>` from the CWS dashboard** (it's the "Item ID" of your item; visible even while unlisted/in review).

That's normal when you distribute both ways. (If you'd rather have *one* ID everywhere, the pro move is to drop the `key` and instead adopt the store's public key in the manifest — more setup; happy to walk through it, but not needed to ship.)

## On "stale"
The previously-uploaded zip differed from this fresh one **only by the `key` field**, which the store ignores anyway — it already had the connect-calendar code and the bifrost URL. So your store listing wasn't broken, but upload this fresh one to be clean and current.

## Version note
If 0.11.5 was already **published**, CWS will reject re-uploading the same version — you'd need to bump (e.g. 0.11.6). If it's still a **draft/in review**, re-uploading 0.11.5 just replaces the draft. Want me to bump the patch version and rebuild the store zip so you're safe either way?

## What changed and how to build each kind going forward
The build now branches on `WXT_STORE_BUILD`:
- **Store zip** → `WXT_STORE_BUILD=1 … npm run zip` → **no key** (store uses its own ID).
- **Friend / load-unpacked** → plain `npm run zip` → **keeps the `babflij…` key** (stable ID). Your existing `auxilio-visitor-share.zip` is unaffected and still valid.

## Redirect URI reminder (now that the store build has no key)
The store-installed extension will use the **store's Item ID**, not `babflij…`. So for store sign-in to work, register:
```
https://<store-item-id>.chromiumapp.org/
```
Get `<store-item-id>` from the CWS dashboard (your item's **Item ID** — visible now, even before it's approved). You'll have **two** redirect URIs registered on the OAuth client:
- `https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org/` → your/friend's load-unpacked build
- `https://<store-item-id>.chromiumapp.org/` → store-installed users

Once this 0.11.6 zip uploads cleanly, grab that Item ID and add its redirect URI — then store-installed sign-in will work the same way the load-unpacked one does (assuming the engine is deployed with the secret + your account is allowed by the consent screen).

content.text:
Found the footgun — and it matters for your dev workflow. The plain build gives a **mismatch**:
- Baked engine URL (from `.env` via `import.meta.env`) = **ngrok** ✓
- `host_permissions` (from `process.env` in `wxt.config.ts`) = **bifrost** ✗ (fallback)

So WXT inlines `.env` into the bundle but does **not** put it into `process.env` for the config — meaning a plain `npm run dev` would call the ngrok engine while only bifrost is in `host_permissions`, and the calls get blocked. Let me fix the config so both read from `.env` consistently.

content.text:
Fixed and verified — both sources now agree:
- Plain build (reads `.env`=ngrok): baked URL **and** `host_permissions` both = ngrok ✓
- Inline `env WXT_ENGINE_URL=…` still overrides (→ bifrost) ✓
- Key still present → ID `babflij…` ✓

## For constant development (live reload)

1. Set the engine in **`.env`** → `WXT_ENGINE_URL=` whichever engine you're iterating against (your local tunnel, or the bifrost URL). It currently points at your ngrok tunnel.
2. Run:
   ```
   npm run dev
   ```
   → outputs to **`.output/chrome-mv3-dev`** and auto-rebuilds + reloads on every save.
3. In `chrome://extensions` (Developer mode on) → **Load unpacked** → select `.output/chrome-mv3-dev` **once**. WXT reloads it for you on changes.
4. **Verify the ID reads `babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn`** the first time (the `key` is in the config, so dev builds include it → stable ID → matches your registered redirect URI). If you ever see a different ID, that build didn't get the key — reload from the right folder.

The stale `chrome-mv3-dev` from 23 Jun (no key, gave `phlplh…`) gets overwritten the moment you run `npm run dev`, so that problem is gone.

## Cheat-sheet: which file for what

| Purpose | Command | Load / upload |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant dev (live reload)** | `npm run dev` | `.output/chrome-mv3-dev` (keyed → `babflij…`) |
| One-off prod-like manual test | `npm run build` | `.output/chrome-mv3` (keyed) |
| **Share with friend** | already built | `auxilio-visitor-share.zip` (keyed) |
| **Chrome Web Store** | `WXT_STORE_BUILD=1 … npm run zip` | `.output/auxilio-extension-0.11.6-chrome.zip` (no key) |

Reminder for dev sign-in to actually work: the dev ID is `babflij…`, so its redirect `https://babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn.chromiumapp.org/` must be registered on the OAuth client (the one you're already adding), and your `.env` engine must be running with the `/addon/*` routes + secret.

One nuance worth confirming once: WXT's *dev* manifest occasionally differs from the build manifest. If after `npm run dev` the ID isn't `babflij…`, tell me and I'll pin it for dev mode too — but I expect it to be fine since the key isn't gated behind `WXT_STORE_BUILD`.

content.text:
Done — Finder should have just popped open with the store zip **selected** (no Cmd+Shift+G needed). Two new scripts now do this for you:

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `npm run zip:store` | Builds the **store** zip (no key, bifrost URL, v0.11.6) **and** reveals it selected in Finder — ready to drag into the CWS upload. |
| `npm run reveal` | Just reveals the newest `.output/*-chrome.zip` in Finder (or opens `.output` if none). Handy after any build. |

So your store workflow is now a single command: **`npm run zip:store`** → Finder opens with the file highlighted → drag it to the Web Store (or click upload and it's already selected in the dialog).

For one-offs without the scripts, the same trick works directly:
- `open -R .output/auxilio-extension-0.11.6-chrome.zip` — reveal that exact file selected
- `open .output` — open the hidden folder
- **Cmd+Shift+.** in any Finder window — toggle hidden files on/off

(Heads-up: `zip:store` hardcodes the bifrost engine URL + `WXT_STORE_BUILD=1`, and pulls the OAuth client id + magic address from `.env` — so keep `.env`'s `WXT_MAGIC_ADDRESS`/`WXT_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` at their production values when you cut a store build. Want me to hardcode those in the script too so a changed `.env` can never leak into a store build?)

3. First load: confirm the ID shows `babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn`.

## What changed
- `wxt.config.ts` → `outDir: 'output'` (was the hidden `.output`).
- All builds/zips now land in **`output/`**: `output/chrome-mv3` (build), `output/chrome-mv3-dev` (dev), `output/*-chrome.zip` (store/zip).
- Deleted the old hidden `.output`, updated `.gitignore` and the `reveal`/`zip:store` scripts to the new path.

So across the board now:
- **Dev:** `npm run dev` → load `output/chrome-mv3-dev`
- **Store upload:** `npm run zip:store` → builds + reveals `output/auxilio-extension-0.11.6-chrome.zip` in Finder

content.text:
That redirect is `https://**cindjjpjkhdmdfnpigbbgcioiikoecbk**.chromiumapp.org/` — a *third* ID, which means you loaded a **no-key build**. Here's the trap you hit: the **`0.11.6` store zip** (`output/auxilio-extension-0.11.6-chrome.zip`) is built with `WXT_STORE_BUILD=1`, which **deliberately strips the key** (the store requires that). Load-unpacking it → Chrome invents a path-based ID (`cindjj…`) → not registered.

**The store zip is only for uploading. For Load unpacked you must use a *keyed* build.** Let me rebuild a keyed one and confirm the ID.

input.command:
bash -c '
set -e
cd /Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/auxilio-extension
echo "=== keyed build for load-unpacked (engine=bifrost, key INCLUDED) ==="
env WXT_ENGINE_URL=https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site npm run build >/dev/null 2>&1
node -e "const m=require(\"./output/chrome-mv3/manifest.json\"); console.log(\"has key:\", !!m.key)"
# compute the ID this build will get
node -e "process.stdout.write(require(\"./output/chrome-mv3/manifest.json\").key||\"\")" | openssl base64 -d -A > /tmp/k.der 2>/dev/null
ID=$(openssl dgst -sha256 -hex /tmp/k.der | sed "s/.*= //" | cut -c1-32 | tr "0-9a-f" "a-p")
echo "this build will load as ID: $ID"
echo
echo ">>> LOAD THIS FOLDER:  output/chrome-mv3"
'

content.text:
Confirmed: the freshly rebuilt **`output/chrome-mv3`** is keyed and will load as **`babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn`** — the ID you registered.

## Do this now
1. `chrome://extensions` → **remove** the `cindjj…` one you just loaded.
2. **Load unpacked** → select:
   ```
   auxilio-extension/output/chrome-mv3
   ```
3. Confirm the card shows ID **`babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn`**.
4. Retry sign-in — the redirect now matches your registered URI.

## The rule that avoids this (it's bitten you 3× now)
**Keyed = load-unpacked. No-key = store upload only.** Never load-unpack the store zip.

| Build | Key? | ID | Use for |
|---|---|---|---|
| `npm run dev` → `output/chrome-mv3-dev` | ✅ | `babflij…` | **Load unpacked / dev** |
| `npm run build` → `output/chrome-mv3` | ✅ | `babflij…` | Load unpacked (one-off) |
| `npm run zip:store` → `…-chrome.zip` | ❌ | path-based | **Web Store upload only** |

⚠️ **One footgun to know:** `npm run zip:store` also rewrites `output/chrome-mv3` *without* the key (that's how it makes the store zip). So if you run `zip:store` and then load `output/chrome-mv3`, you'll get a wrong ID again. To stay safe, do your load-unpacked testing from **`output/chrome-mv3-dev`** (run `npm run dev`) — that folder is keyed and is *never* touched by the store build.

So going forward: **`npm run dev` → load `output/chrome-mv3-dev`** for all device testing. Right now, since I just built it keyed, `output/chrome-mv3` also works for an immediate retry.

content.text:
That redirect is `https://**phlplhnahmohnbfdfcemdogompigafjm**.chromiumapp.org/` — and since you installed it **from the store**, that's the **store's own Item ID**. It's *different* from `babflij…` (which is only the load-unpacked ID). You registered `babflij…`, but the store extension is `phlplh…`, so it's not registered → mismatch.

This is exactly the "store assigns its own ID, register that one too" point. The store ID was never going to be `babflij` because store builds have no key.

## Fix
In Google Cloud Console → **APIs & Services → Credentials** → OAuth client **`561888594292-gag466…`** (must be this exact client) → **Authorized redirect URIs**, add:
```
https://phlplhnahmohnbfdfcemdogompigafjm.chromiumapp.org/
https://phlplhnahmohnbfdfcemdogompigafjm.chromiumapp.org
```
Save.

## Checklist if it still errors right after
1. **Right client?** Confirm you're editing client id `561888594292-gag466rotigi0rs62urc17abhgfpidsj` — the same one baked into the extension. Adding it to a different client/project won't help.
2. **Propagation delay.** Google OAuth changes aren't always instant — wait **a few minutes** after saving, then retry (and fully close/reopen the sign-in popup).
3. **Exact string.** Copy it straight from the error message; that's the canonical value Chrome sends.

## Where this leaves you (two IDs, both valid)
You're distributing two ways, so you keep **both** redirect URIs registered on that one OAuth client:

| Install method | Extension ID | Redirect URI to register |
|---|---|---|
| **Store-installed** (published) | `phlplhnahmohnbfdfcemdogompigafjm` | `https://phlplh…chromiumapp.org/` |
| **Load unpacked** (you/friend) | `babflijdehjlajekidajimhaggoceabn` | `https://babflij…chromiumapp.org/` |

Add the `phlplh…` one now and the store install will sign in (assuming the consent screen allows your account, and the engine has the secret + routes for the post-sign-in calls). The store Item ID is also shown in your CWS dashboard if you ever need to re-confirm it.

content.text:
Good — that grep clears one thing up: the **scopes are correct**. Your current source *does* request `contacts.readonly` + `contacts.other.readonly` (for the People API name/photo resolver), so the published 0.11.6 matches your code. Not stale, not the problem.

So this is **purely** the redirect URI still not being registered *as Google sees it right now*. The error is unambiguous: for client `561888…gag466`, Google has no `https://phlplhnahmohnbfdfcemdogompigafjm.chromiumapp.org/` in its redirect list yet. Since you say you added it, one of these is true — check in order:

1. **Wrong client.** The request's `client_id` is `561888594292-gag466rotigi0rs62urc17abhgfpidsj`. In Cloud Console → Credentials, open the OAuth client whose ID matches **that exact string**. If you have more than one OAuth client (or more than one project), it's easy to edit the wrong one. The client must also be type **Web application**.

2. **Wrong field.** It must go under **Authorized redirect URIs**, not **Authorized JavaScript origins**. They're two separate boxes on the same page; the JS-origins one won't fix this.

3. **Exact string + trailing slash.** Paste it verbatim from the error:
   ```
   https://phlplhnahmohnbfdfcemdogompigafjm.chromiumapp.org/
   ```
   A missing trailing slash, a typo, or `http` vs `https` all count as a mismatch.

4. **Saved + persisted.** Hit **Save** at the bottom, then **reload the credentials page** and confirm the URI is actually listed. (Easy to add a line and navigate away without saving.)

5. **Propagation.** Google says redirect-URI changes can take **5 minutes to a few hours** to take effect. If you retried immediately, that alone explains it. Wait, then retry in a **fresh** sign-in popup (or an incognito window to dodge caching).

**Quick way to tell if it's propagation vs. config:** once the URI is truly on the right client, the error *changes* — instead of `redirect_uri_mismatch` you'll get the consent screen (or an "unverified app / access blocked" screen because of the sensitive calendar+contacts scopes). If you still see `redirect_uri_mismatch` after ~10 minutes, it's not on the right client/field yet (revisit 1–4).

If you can tell me what the credentials page shows — the client's ID, its type, and the exact redirect URIs listed — I can pinpoint which of the five it is.
